introduction.purpose:
This Software Requirements Specification (SRS) defines the operational requirements for the **Gemini 8m Telescopes Control System** software. The goal is to provide a functional tool for guiding the development of controls and data acquisition systems in a manner consistent with their operation within the Gemini System. It establishes both general criteria and specific functional requirements for software and controls design in the Gemini Project. The document is oriented toward developers of control and data acquisition systems, not toward the science user of the Gemini telescopes. It logically follows the Operational Concepts document and covers the impacts of science requirements and control system design requirements.

Document Number: SPE-C-G0014. Copyright 1994 by Gemini 8-m Telescopes Project.
introduction.documentConventions: This document follows IEEE 830-1998 conventions for SRS documents. It draws heavily in form and general content from ESO's VLT Software Requirements Specification (VLT-SPE-ESO-10000-11). The document uses a formal numbering scheme for requirements organized into General Description (Ch2), General Requirements (Ch3), Specific Requirements (Ch4), and Commands (Ch5). Action items for unresolved issues are tracked at the document front. Operational levels (observing, maintenance, test) and access modes (observing, monitoring, operation, planning, testing, administrative) are formally defined with specific user-level mappings. Inline reviewer comments are preserved for traceability (e.g., 'I think you should include Rick's information about complexity criteria here — Steven'). Permission identifiers use hierarchical privilege and protection levels aligned with MIL-STD standards.
introduction.intendedAudience: This document is intended for developers of Gemini telescope control and data acquisition systems, EPICS (Experimental Physics and Industrial Control System) developers working on IOC software, the Gemini Software Design Team and work package managers, operations staff including telescope operators and science observers, system architects designing the distributed control architecture spanning multiple observatory facilities, and quality assurance personnel verifying compliance with safety and reliability requirements.
introduction.productScope:
The **Gemini 8m Telescopes Control System** software encompasses all software directly involved in the control of Gemini systems, including commercial packages used for that purpose. Software categories include: (A) Developed software — non-commercial software developed for telescope and instrumentation control; (B) Supported software — commercial or public-domain software used for telescope control with interface specifications; (C) External software — user-supplied data reduction tools, operating systems, and CASE tools with interface requirements only.

The system controls two 8-meter telescopes located on Mauna Kea (Hawaii) and Cerro Pachon (Chile), supporting multi-instrument operation (3 instruments at cassegrain focus), multi-user concurrent access, and remote operations from 7 facility types. Four observing modes are supported: interactive, queue-based (primary mode), remote operations, and service observing. The system is built on EPICS (Experimental Physics and Industrial Control System) as the foundation, using a Virtual Observatory System (VOS) architecture with Virtual System Interface (VSI) abstractions.

All software is maintained under a version control system (CVS) and must include facilities for easy inclusion into the Gemini software environment.
references: SPE-PS-G001: Gemini Science Requirements, Version 1.1 (November 11, 1992)
SPE-C-G0026: Goals and Requirements for Software and Controls (August 23, 1993)
SPE-C-G0015: Operational Concept Definition (April 26, 1993)
PG-C-G0005: Software and Control Management Plan (April 5, 1993)
SPE-C-G0037: Software Design Specification (April 20, 1994)
SPE-C-G0011: Software Configuration Control Plan (August 23, 1993)
SPE-ASA-G0008: Gemini Electronic Design Specification (June 21, 1994)
ESO VLT Software Requirements Specification, Issue 1.0 (VLT-SPE-ESO-10000-11)
MIL-STD-822B: System Safety Program Requirements
MIL-STD-1309C: Definitions of Terms for Test, Measurement and Diagnostic Equipment
NOST 100-1.0: Definition of the Flexible Image Transport System (FITS)
POSIX Programmer's Guide by Donald Lewine, O'Reilly & Associates, Inc., 1991

overallDescription.productPerspective:
The **Gemini Control System** is a distributed telescope control and data acquisition system designed for two 8-meter optical/infrared telescopes — one on Mauna Kea (Hawaii) and one on Cerro Pachon (Chile). The system replaces traditional interactive telescope control with a primarily queue-based, automated observing environment.

Key architectural concepts:
- **Virtual Observatory System (VOS)**: A design model that minimizes intersystem dependencies and hides subsystem implementation details from system-level operations.
- **Virtual System Interface (VSI)**: An abstraction layer within VOS that isolates implementation-specific interface requirements.
- **EPICS Foundation**: The Experimental Physics and Industrial Control System from Los Alamos and Argonne National Laboratories provides the IOC (Input/Output Control) infrastructure, channel access protocol, database scanning mechanisms, and standard OPI tools.

The system operates across a hierarchy of facilities: Telescope Enclosure, Control Facility (co-located), Site Support Facility, Base Facility, Remote Operations Facility (super sites), Satellite Site Facility, and Institutional Site Facility. Communication spans LANs (Ethernet IEEE 802.3 for control, FDDI for backbone), WAN links (T1 minimum for remote operations), and specialized buses (Local Time Bus, digital reflective memory bus, analog event-based bus).

The two telescopes operate independently; there are no plans for concurrent use as a single facility, though future interferometry with nearby telescopes is a long-term goal.
productFunctions: Multi-instrument telescope control: Parallel access to 3 science instruments at cassegrain focus with one active instrument receiving the telescope beam. Inactive instruments can take calibrations, prepare for hot standby, or operate at any level independently.
Queue-based observing: Primary observation mode supporting fully automated science programs, flexible scheduling, and efficient telescope utilization across multiple observing programs.
Interactive observing: Direct observation through the Observatory Control System (OCS) sequencer with break/resequence capability for observation queues.
Remote operations: Full-featured remote observing, monitoring, control, diagnostics, and eavesdropping from multiple facility types with bandwidth-adaptive capability.
Service observing: Staff astronomers perform data collection on behalf of remote astronomers, requiring well-specified computer-executable observing programs.
Science program planning: Virtual telescope simulator for offline testing of observing sequences, completeness verification, and observation parameter estimation without actual telescope access.

Data acquisition and archiving: Detector data acquisition with loss-less compression, FITS format storage, automatic archiving to Gemini Archive, 7-day online data retention, and interactive access to archived data.
Multi-user concurrent access: Support for up to 6 active control nodes and 2 monitoring nodes (1 local, 1 remote) simultaneously, scalable to 10 active nodes.
Command routing and control: Three routing methods (Direct, Channel with intelligent conversion, Broadcast) with ACK/NAK protocol, 500ms timeout, command handshaking within 100-200ms for IOCs.
Safety monitoring: Multi-level protection system (software limits, soft/hard limit switches, software/hardware watchdogs, hardware interlocks, hard stops) with self-monitoring for risk prevention.
System diagnostics and self-test: Built-in test (BIT) facilities, regression testing, monitor-level background tasks, self-test sequences, and system-level integration tests.
Flexible scheduling: Dynamic observation queue resorting based on weather conditions, target positions, instrument configurations, and observatory directorate rules.

userClassesAndCharacteristics.characteristics: End user of the Gemini system for science data collection. Range from occasional users to very experienced staff astronomers. Wish to concentrate on efficient data acquisition and on-line data quality assessment. Have no direct telescope control privileges — interact through observing programs and the sequencer. Access modes: planning, observing, monitoring. Typically submit commands to a queue via user interface.

On-site person responsible for monitoring data acquisition, validating data integrity for the astronomer, and ensuring the science plan functions correctly. Same interface requirements as the astronomer. Access modes: observing, monitoring.

On-site controller of telescope and instruments. Responsible for system integrity and accurate operation during observations. Works with the Observer and science plan. Very experienced users familiar with the telescope and instruments. Can access direct control commands and maintenance procedures. Access modes: observing, monitoring, operation, testing. Often accesses multiple modes simultaneously.

On-site or near-site personnel responsible for hardware and software maintenance, installation of subsystems, and configuration changes. Must have full access to all subsystems. Access modes: monitoring, operation, testing. Work at maintenance and test operational levels.

Responsible for designing, testing, configuring, and upgrading subsystems. May be based at the Gemini site, base facility, remote operations facility, or at an associated institute. Have deep understanding of Gemini software. Require highest privilege to modify everything in the system. Usually work at test level. Access modes: monitoring, testing.

Responsible for high-level functional control of the integrated Gemini system. Determine observation scheduling changes, maintenance down-time, and system modifications. Simple, safe, status-inquiry-only interface. No direct control capability. Access modes: monitoring, administrative.

overallDescription.operatingEnvironment:
The Gemini Control System operates across two observatory sites: Mauna Kea (Hawaii) and Cerro Pachon (Chile). The development operating system is UNIX System V Release 4, using only POSIX-compliant calls. The host workstation OS is Solaris 2.3 or above. The IOC (Input/Output Control) layer uses VxWorks real-time operating system from Wind River Systems with EPICS as the software framework. IOC hardware is VME/VXI-based chassis with Motorola 68040 processors (MVME-167B at 33MHz) with at least 16MB RAM.

GUI development uses X-windows (X11R5 or X11R6) with Tcl/Tk as the principle language. Development tools include GNU software suite, Imake or GNU autoconf, CVS for version control, and GNU cc compiler for IOC cross-compilation. Communication uses DARPA TCP/IP protocol over LANs and WANs, supporting standard ARPA services (telnet, FTP, SMTP), NFS, RPC, IPC, and Unix socket interface. The LAN must support 20-40 Mbits/second transfer rate. Network architecture uses Ethernet IEEE 802.3 for control LAN, FDDI for backbone LAN, and specialized buses for time distribution, digital reflective memory, and analog events.

Developer workstations are SUN SPARC-10 (model 41) or SPARC Classic with 64MB RAM, at least 2GB disk, DDS DAT cartridges, and CD-ROM. Communication between workstations and IOCs uses DRAMA's IMP protocol with SDS command structure. Inter-IOC communication uses EPICS Channel Access protocol. Quick-look analysis uses PV-Wave/IDL. Off-line data reduction packages include ADAM, IRAF, Midas, IDL, and Khoros.
designAndImplementationConstraints: All Gemini software must be developed using standard methodologies and development environments with all components easily (preferably automatically) combined into an integrated system.
Individual instruments must run fully independently; telescope software at both sites must be identical in upper layers even if hardware differs.
Adding new instruments should aim to introduce no modification to already operational parts; modifications should be confined to operational procedures.
Commercial packages, off-the-shelf public domain software, and standards must be used whenever feasible to decrease life-cycle costs.
All software not directly controlling specific hardware must be written as machine-independent, portable code; even microprocessor software should be hardware-independent.
Table-driven software should be used to avoid unnecessary compilations; system constants must not require recompiling.
Gemini developers must maintain accurate change logs and adhere to standard error reporting/recording methods.
All software must be version labeled in source and binary form, with version information retrievable from executing software via control commands.
Software must be developed using CVS version control, with on-line version control available to recover/restore versions at all times.
Only POSIX-compliant operating system calls are permitted; vendor-specific extensions are not allowed.
All subsystem packages must include a simulator module for the virtual telescope and user interface modules for all operating environments.
All accesses to control electronics hardware must be through VME IOCs using EPICS software; no other control interfaces are permitted.
Peer-to-peer connectivity should only be used to overcome demonstrated performance problems.
Due to the uncertain future of the Internet, only non-essential tasks may employ it; all essential tasks must use project-controlled resources.
userDocumentation: All Gemini software must be fully documented with appropriate internal comments and external documentation including Unix-style man pages.
A supportability plan will be part of the Gemini Control System addressing maintenance needs, personnel availability, downtime, and standardization.
A Controls Test Plan will address all areas of testing from design through acceptance, commissioning, and hand-over.
assumptionsAndDependencies: The hosting system and servers, primarily based on SUN SPARC workstations and Motorola VME IOCs, support all required control and data acquisition operations.
EPICS community software support is available nominally free-of-charge through normal release and bug-fix procedures.
The Gemini Science Requirements (SPE-PS-G001) establish the observing performance requirements that drive the control system design.
Remote site bandwidth will determine the level of remote operations capability available at each location.
The Ward/Mellor approach to developing real-time systems is used for analysis, design, and development.

externalInterfaceRequirements.userInterfaces:
The Gemini user interface defines how users see the system. A homogeneous user interface is achieved by applying the same UI tools across the entire project, providing a consistent 'look and feel'. The UI is network-transparent and callable from multiple stations regardless of location. Different access modes present different look-and-feels while maintaining uniformity across subsystems.

User interface tools are based on standards that are portable across different hardware platforms (Portable User Interface Toolkit). GUI systems use X-windows (X11R5/R6) with Tcl/Tk as the principle development language. The UI provides: simple and safe controls grouped for convenience (astronomers), organized controls for quick response to unsafe conditions with ability to monitor all subsystems (telescope operators), full subsystem access (support/developers), and simple status inquiries (administrators).

The system supports concurrent multi-station operation with different access levels at each station. Any subsystem should be accessible and controllable from any single point with appropriate protection and security.
externalInterfaceRequirements.hardwareInterfaces:
Standard interfaces to control electronics are provided through: standard VME bus systems, a standard set of interface cards used across all subsystems and instruments, and a standard software skeleton running on IOCs. IOC crates use Motorola MVME-167B (68040@33MHz) processors in VME/VXI chassis with at least 16MB RAM.

Detector data hardware must support maximum acceptable readout times: approximately 0.1 sec for focusing (partial detector reads) and 2-3 minutes for full readout of mosaicked large optical detectors. Equipment at the telescope site must meet altitude and humidity specifications. Computer hardware must provide identical internal data representation across all platforms.

The interlock system provides hardware-independent safety protection: hard-wired stop buttons, electrical interlocks, hard/soft limit switches, and mechanical hard stops with dampers.
externalInterfaceRequirements.softwareInterfaces:
The Gemini software interfaces with: (1) EPICS Channel Access protocol for IOC-to-IOC and IOC-to-workstation communication, (2) DRAMA's IMP protocol with SDS command structure for workstation-to-IOC control communication, (3) Commercial DBMS for operational information (schedules, logs, problem reports, maintenance), (4) Star catalogs via STARCAT (Space Telescope Archive and Catalog) for guide/standard star selection, (5) FITS format for astronomical data storage and transport, (6) Image processing packages (ADAM, IRAF, Midas, IDL, Khoros) for off-line data reduction, (7) PV-Wave/IDL for quick-look analysis, (8) Archiving system for automatic data archival, (9) VxWorks real-time OS and EPICS for IOC environments, (10) Visitor instrument interfaces providing standardized subset of Gemini facilities.

The EPICS software framework provides core components: Channel Access (client/server), Database (memory-resident), Scanners (periodic, event, I/O event, passive), Monitors, DCT, Build Tools, and Source/Release management.
externalInterfaceRequirements.communicationsInterfaces:
The system requires multi-layer communication infrastructure:

**LAN**: Ethernet IEEE 802.3 for control LAN supporting 20-40 Mbits/second. FDDI backbone LAN for higher bandwidth requirements. Supplemented by Local Time Bus for time signal distribution, digital reflective memory bus, and analog event-based bus.

**WAN**: At least one dedicated T1 bandwidth link for remote support, development, and testing. Standard ARPA services (telnet, FTP, SMTP), NFS, RPC, IPC, Unix sockets supported. Communication hardware must use DARPA TCP/IP protocol.

**Protocols**: ACK/NAK command acknowledgment within 500ms timeout. IOC command handshaking within 100-200ms. Peak control information rate of 100 TPS with network bridging. Video transmission: rough images every 0.5 sec (data-loss compression acceptable), high-quality images within 20 sec (loss-less compression only).

**Network Requirements**: Homogeneous LAN/WAN interfaces based on standards allowing media migration. Hierarchical ISO/OSI model required with exceptions only for demonstrated performance needs. Network redundancy considered for reliability and security. Single-point failure protection through double-loop or re-routing capabilities.

systemFeatures.name: Observatory Control System (OCS)
systemFeatures.description: The OCS is the highest-level software component responsible for coordinating all telescope operations. It provides the command sequencer that interprets Science Programs, manages the observation queue, and dispatches programs to subsystems. The OCS supports all four observing modes (interactive, queue-based, remote, service) and manages the flexible scheduling system that optimizes telescope utilization based on weather, target positions, and instrument configurations. All commands within OCS are represented as ASCII strings with labeled fields and permitted nesting using braces. Priority: Critical.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: Astronomer submits a science program via the user interface. Response: OCS validates the program, adds it to the observation queue, and acknowledges acceptance within 2 seconds.

Stimulus: Scheduler determines current conditions require queue resorting. Response: OCS resorts the observation queue based on target positions, weather conditions, instrument configurations, and observatory directorate rules, then dispatches the optimal program to the sequencer.

Stimulus: Telescope Operator requests direct interactive control during queue-based observing. Response: OCS enables direct interactive operation mode while maintaining observation logging and safety monitoring.

Stimulus: Remote observer submits commands via remote user interface. Response: OCS routes commands through the scheduler transparently, with functionality identical to local operation (speed may vary with link bandwidth).
functionalRequirements: The OCS shall provide a sequencer that interprets computer-executable Science Programs for near-automatic observation execution.
The OCS shall support breaking and resequencing the observation queue at any time, including as a result of data quality assessment.
The OCS shall manage flexible scheduling software that advises operations staff on optimal observation choices compatible with relative program priorities.
The OCS shall support interleaving of observing programs transparently to individual programs during queue-based operation.
The OCS shall represent all commands as ASCII strings with Command_ID (monotonically increasing for timestamping), Source, Target, Opcode, and Parameter set fields.
The OCS shall support three command routing methods: Direct (to specific subsystem), Channel (with optional intelligent data conversion), and Broadcast (to all subsystems).
The OCS shall provide a pass-through mode for initial sequencer implementation with minimal checking and delay.

systemFeatures.name: Instrument Control System (ICS)
systemFeatures.description: The ICS provides comprehensive control of all scientific instruments mounted at the telescope's cassegrain focus. The system supports parallel access to multiple instruments while ensuring only one receives the telescope beam. Inactive instruments can independently take calibrations, prepare for hot standby, or undergo testing. The system supports both Gemini-standard instruments and visitor instruments through a standardized interface subset. Priority: Critical.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: OCS sends instrument configuration command for next observation. Response: ICS configures the target instrument within the 2-minute startup window, reporting readiness status.
Stimulus: Beam switching requested between instruments. Response: ICS performs dynamic reconfiguration without restarting instruments or telescope, ensuring no adverse impact on any instrument.

Stimulus: Visitor instrument connects to Gemini system. Response: ICS provides standardized interface supporting status acquisition, preprogrammed observing sequences, telescope position/focus offsets, and optional coordinate transforms and archiving.
functionalRequirements: The system shall provide parallel access to all mounted instruments, with one active instrument receiving the telescope beam.
Inactive instruments shall be able to take calibration/flat field exposures in parallel and prepare for hot standby.
No permitted action of an inactive instrument shall adversely impact the active instrument.
Instrument startup from end of telescope startup shall take 2 minutes or less.
Visitor instruments must operate as servers responding to commands from upper system levels.
The visitor instrument interface shall support: status acquisition, preprogrammed observing sequences, telescope position/focus offsets.
The visitor instrument interface should be a stable, long-lived subset of the existing instrumentation interface.

systemFeatures.name: Telescope Control System (TCS)
systemFeatures.description: The TCS provides direct control of the telescope mount, enclosure, and optical systems. It is responsible for detecting and invoking parallel operation across telescope and enclosure subsystems for efficiency. The TCS works under the direction of the OCS and can be controlled by the Telescope Operator in direct operation mode. Start-up sequences progress through cold start (5 min with software download), warm start (1 min without download), and telescope start (4 min from end of cold/warm start). Priority: Critical.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: Cold start command issued. Response: System downloads all software and initializes within approximately 5 minutes. Telescope startup follows in approximately 4 additional minutes.

Stimulus: Telescope Operator issues direct slew command. Response: TCS validates the command against software limits, executes the slew, and provides status updates within 4 seconds at local stations.

Stimulus: Safety sensor detects hazardous condition. Response: TCS invokes multi-level safety system (software limits first, then soft limit switches, watchdogs, hard limits, interlocks, hard stops) to bring telescope to safe state.
functionalRequirements: Cold startup including software download shall take approximately 5 minutes; warm startup approximately 1 minute; telescope startup approximately 4 minutes.
The TCS shall detect and invoke parallel operation across telescope and enclosure subsystems.
Every command must be accepted or rejected within 2 seconds before the corresponding action occurs.
Status display updates must occur within 4 seconds at local stations.
Requests for subsystem status information must be answered within 5 seconds.

The system shall implement the 7-level safety hierarchy: software limits, soft limit switches, software watchdogs, hardware watchdogs, hard limit switches, hardware interlocks, and mechanical hard stops.
The system shall constantly monitor active and inactive subsystems for correct operation.

systemFeatures.name: Data Handling System (DHS)
systemFeatures.description: The DHS manages the complete lifecycle of astronomical data from detector readout through archival. It handles detector data acquisition, temporary storage, compression, quality assessment, and transfer to the Gemini Archive. The system supports multiple data formats and compression strategies, maintains 7-day online data retention, and provides quick-look analysis capability for real-time data quality assessment. Priority: Critical.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: Detector completes readout. Response: DHS acquires data, stores uncompressed copy locally for data security, compresses using loss-less algorithm, transfers to system data disk for quality assessment, and initiates automatic archival.

Stimulus: Observer requests quick-look data analysis. Response: DHS provides synchronous quick-look processing using PV-Wave/IDL with preprocessed data in common format, feeding results and parameters back to control software.
Stimulus: Remote astronomer requests data transfer to home institute. Response: DHS transmits data in FITS format with all header information across WAN link.
functionalRequirements: Detector data shall be stored using the most effective method permitted by available technology, with minimum intermediate storage.
At least two copies of data must be maintained: one to secure data as acquired, one for on-line quality assessment (preferably on removable media).
Data capacity must retain 7 days of data from the largest instrument, with the last 3 days available interactively from hard disk.
Loss-less compression shall be used for data transmission across LAN/WAN and for archival storage.
Data transport between Gemini and home institutes shall use FITS format with complete header information.
Quick-look analysis shall be synchronous and usable within exposure sequences for programmatic feedback without manual intervention.
Near-line processing shall be available for data integrity validation, running asynchronously from data acquisition with acquisition taking precedence.
All astronomical data shall be automatically archived during observing and maintenance level operation.

systemFeatures.name: Remote Operations System
systemFeatures.description: The Remote Operations System enables full telescope control, observing, monitoring, diagnostics, and testing from remote facilities. The system provides bandwidth-adaptive capability ranging from full remote control (high-speed links) to voice-only communication. All software is designed from inception to support remote operations with no conceptual difference between local and remote operation. The system supports team observing with multiple observers at different sites. Priority: High.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: Remote observer at super site initiates observation. Response: System provides full control and monitoring capability from remote workstation with performance transparent to the observer (link speed determines perceived transparency).
Stimulus: Remote diagnostics requested from base facility. Response: System provides monitoring and diagnostic access with multi-point monitoring capability and interactive status examination.

Stimulus: Remote observer requests eavesdropping on current observation. Response: System duplicates observer's screen at remote site allowing pick-and-choose of displayed information, with no effect from remote keyboard on local environment.
functionalRequirements: All software shall be developed to permit remote operations with no conceptual difference between on-site and remote software.
All observing facilities shall work both on-site and off-site with full remote operations capability.
Team observing with multiple observers at different sites shall be supported.
It shall be possible to restrict specific operations to specific remote sites dynamically and independently of the operations themselves.
Remote monitoring shall allow the remote user to pick and choose displayed information without affecting the local environment.
The system shall provide bandwidth-adaptive capability from full remote control to voice-only communication based on site capabilities.
Remote control shall be restricted to facilities with hard-wired stop button, real-time video and audio, and telescope control capability.

systemFeatures.name: EPICS IOC Framework
systemFeatures.description: The EPICS (Experimental Physics and Industrial Control System) framework provides the foundation for all hardware control in the Gemini system. Each IOC (Input/Output Controller) contains a memory-resident database with extensible record types, database scanning mechanisms (periodic, event, I/O event, passive), record/device/driver support modules, and Channel Access for network-transparent database access. The framework supports database monitors for change notifications and standardized OPI tools (MEDM, DM, ALH, AR, Sequencer) for real-time monitoring and control. Priority: Critical.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: Database record value changes in an IOC. Response: Channel Access server notifies all registered monitoring clients across the network. Database monitors trigger callbacks for value, alarm state, or archive changes as configured.

Stimulus: OPI tool requests database record access. Response: Channel Access client establishes communication with the appropriate IOC server, providing network-transparent access to the record's fields.

Stimulus: New instrument IOC is deployed. Response: IOC boots with VxWorks, loads EPICS database, initializes record/device/driver support, and registers with Channel Access for automatic discovery by OPI clients.
functionalRequirements: All IOCs shall use EPICS as the software infrastructure with memory-resident databases.
Database access times shall be in the range of 2-3 milliseconds per access.
Asynchronous writes shall be supported for concurrent operation.
Time-access critical information shall be available in memory.
There shall be a consistent and logical (name-based) access method.
The database shall support both remote access and distributed data.
Record names must be unique across all IOCs attached to the same TCP/IP subnet.
Four database scanning types shall be supported: Periodic, Event, I/O Event, and Passive.
Channel Access shall provide network-transparent access to IOC databases using a client-server model.

systemFeatures.name: System Self-Test and Diagnostics
systemFeatures.description: Comprehensive built-in test facilities for all Gemini subsystems operating at three levels: Monitor (continuous background checking), Self-test (full component exercise on startup and demand), and System-level (integrated testing during maintenance). Each subsystem includes a simulator for virtual telescope operation and maintenance testing. The goal is 90% fault detection rate before impact on observing. Priority: High.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: System startup begins. Response: Each subsystem automatically executes self-test sequence, exercises all hardware and software components, and reports results (OK/BAD/WARNING) to OCS.
Stimulus: RunTest command broadcast to all subsystems. Response: Each subsystem performs configured self-test and returns status. Detailed results available via GetConfiguration command.
Stimulus: Monitor-level task detects anomaly in power supply. Response: Background monitoring task reports problem to OCS, which logs the fault with timestamp and notifies appropriate personnel.
functionalRequirements: Every software module shall have corresponding test specifications for acceptance and online testing.
The system shall provide for execution of automatic self-test sequences exercising all subsystems in a given configuration.
Regression tests shall be part of every software package.
Monitor-level background tasks shall run whenever subsystems are operational, checking power supply levels, temperatures, performance, and command responses.
Self-test modules shall be executed automatically during start-up and on demand through the defined interface.
System-level integration tests shall be available for execution on demand during maintenance operation level.
All subsystems shall include a simulator providing reference behavior, with simple mechanisms for substitution.
Fault detection (all means) shall achieve 90% capability; fault detection (BIT) shall achieve 90%; fault isolation to eight or fewer modules shall achieve 95%.

systemFeatures.name: Access Control and Security System
systemFeatures.description: A comprehensive privilege and protection system that dynamically allocates resources and enforces access control based on operational levels, access modes, and user categories. The Access Mode Allocation system prevents deadlocks while protecting critical resources. Security measures prevent both accidental command mix-ups between users and WAN intrusion, with a firewall gateway architecture. All systems are appropriately interlocked with hardware-independent safety protection. Priority: High.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: User logs into the system from a remote site. Response: System authenticates user, determines privilege level based on user category and location, assigns access mode, and presents appropriate interface with matching look-and-feel.
Stimulus: Engineering/Maintenance mode activated on a subsystem. Response: Subsystem ignores directives from other systems while continuing to provide status information.
Stimulus: Unauthorized WAN access attempt detected. Response: Network gateway firewall blocks intrusion attempt, protecting astronomical database and control systems.
functionalRequirements: The system shall implement an Access Mode Allocation system that dynamically identifies and assigns resources, preventing deadlocks.
Privileges shall be determined by user category, operational level, and location (local/remote).
The system shall enforce that Engineering/Maintenance mode subsystems ignore external directives while still providing status.
A network gateway firewall shall protect the Gemini system from WAN intrusion.
There shall be security preventing unauthorized users or users at unauthorized access levels from accessing the system.
The system shall support policy-based restrictions on simultaneous access without hard software limits.
Protection software shall clearly indicate the current operation level to users and check compatibility between subsystems in different modes.

performanceRequirements: Every command must be accepted or rejected within 2 seconds before the corresponding action occurs.
IOC command handshaking must occur within 100-200 milliseconds.
ACK/NAK communication protocol timeout must be approximately 500 milliseconds.
Status display updates must occur within 4 seconds at local stations.
Subsystem status requests must be answered within 5 seconds.
Peak control information rate is 100 TPS (transactions per second) with network bridging for traffic isolation.
LAN must support 20-40 Mbits/second data transfer rate for concurrent data access and display.
Fast video transmission every 0.5 seconds (data-loss compression acceptable); high-quality video within 20 seconds (loss-less compression only).
Detector readout: approximately 0.1 seconds for focusing operations; 2-3 minutes for full mosaicked optical detector readout.
Database access times shall be 2-3 milliseconds per access.
Engineering data logging at up to 200 Hz for short periods; long-term logging at 1 Hz or less.
Cold startup approximately 5 minutes (including software download); warm startup approximately 1 minute; telescope startup approximately 4 minutes; instrument startup 2 minutes or less.
Recovery and/or reconfiguration goal: 5 minutes from onset of error condition to observing again.
safetyRequirements: The system must be self-monitoring to invoke safety monitoring and prevent risk to people or damage to equipment.
Safety protection must be independent of software, using mechanical hard stops, electrical interlocks, hard/soft limit switches, and watchdogs.
The 7-level safety hierarchy must be implemented: software limits, soft limit switches, software watchdogs, hardware watchdogs, hard limit switches, hardware interlocks, hard stops.
All hazards capable of causing death or loss of irreplaceable equipment shall be passively interlocked.
All hazards capable of causing injury or severe damage shall be actively interlocked.
All other hazards may be interlocked via software.
The system shall bring the telescope to a safe state quickly upon detection of danger.
Hard stops shall use dampers to avoid equipment damage.
securityRequirements: Security must prevent accidental mix-up of commands from different users on different system parts.
A well-designed network gateway firewall shall prevent WAN intrusion into the Gemini system.
The astronomical database must be protected from intrusion for both data access and destructive purposes.
User access shall be controlled through privilege levels determined during system login.
Remote operations security may restrict specific operations to specific sites.
softwareQualityAttributes: Simplicity: The system shall be as simple as possible while meeting all requirements, with complexity criteria applied to design decisions.
Supportability: A formal supportability plan shall address maintenance planning, manpower, supply support, equipment, training, facilities, and design interface.

Reliability and Availability: Maximum 2% (goal 1%) total system downtime due to failures — translating to 15 minutes per night or 1 night per month. Retry procedures must be embedded for error recovery.

Maintainability: Maintenance adjustment interval of 30 days or longer. Mean/Maximum Time to Repair for each maintenance level. Minimize downtime, cost, personnel skill levels, maintenance-induced errors.
Human Engineering: Provisions for minimizing stress and fatigue, operational feedback, human-machine interfaces, procedures, training, team interaction.
Testability: 90% fault detection, 95% fault isolation to eight or fewer modules, with built-in test facilities.
Expandability: Software designed for easy extension and upgrade using general industry standards.
Modularity: Strictly modular design where subsystem functionality is self-contained, installable and maintainable independently.
Concurrency: Parallel operation to improve efficiency, with TCS capable of detecting and invoking parallel operations.
businessRules: Queue-based observing is the primary observation mode; interactive observing is supported but not considered normal mode.
Full redundancy is not required, but cost-effective redundant subsystems (e.g., telescope control computers) may be implemented.
Data redundancy is required to prevent single-failure data loss.
Failure conditions shall not cascade — failure of one subsystem shall not affect other working subsystems.
No more than 15% of observing should be preempted for queue scheduling flexibility.
A 10% overhead to the prime observing project is acceptable for picking up nearby observations with identical filters.

otherRequirements: All Gemini software must conform to the development model defined in the Software Management Plan (SMP) and Software Configuration Control Plan (SCP).
The Ward/Mellor approach to real-time system development shall be used, with CASE tools such as TSEE by Westmount Technologies.
An object-oriented approach is encouraged but not required.
COCOMO/effort estimation: Not explicitly provided in the document; project spans multiple years with evolutionary development across distributed international teams.

glossary.definition: Acquisition and Guiding — subsystem for telescope target acquisition and guiding.
A method of allocating resources using both access method and access privileges, enabling concurrent multi-user operation with dynamic resource assignment.
Communication protocol mandating positive or negative acknowledgment to all messages.
Computer-Aided Software Engineering tools used for functional analysis.
Command Line User Interface.
Database Management System for operational information storage.
Allowing an observer to watch an observation from a secondary site with no control capability.
Experimental Physics and Industrial Control System — toolkit from Los Alamos and Argonne National Laboratories providing the foundation of the Gemini control system.
Flexible Image Transport System — standard data format for astronomical data storage and transport.
Graphical User Interface, implemented via X-windows (X11R5/R6) and Tcl/Tk.
High-level Operations Software.
Instrument Control Software.
Input/Output Control Module — VME/VXI-based chassis with Motorola 68xxx processor running VxWorks and EPICS for hardware control.
Interprocess Communication.
Local Area Network — Ethernet IEEE 802.3 for control, FDDI for backbone.
Observatory Control Software — highest-level control component managing observation scheduling and command dispatch.
Relational Database Management System for configuration, logging, and maintenance data.
A formal description of an observer's plan for using the Gemini telescopes, suitable for near-automatic execution by the sequencer.
The command interpreter for Science Programs and telescope control within the OCS.
Space Telescope Archive and Catalog — interface for astronomical object catalog access.
Telescope Control Software — controls telescope mount, enclosure, and optical systems.
Virtual Observatory System — design model minimizing intersystem dependencies and hiding subsystem implementation details.
Virtual System Interface — abstraction layer within VOS isolating implementation-specific interface requirements.
Wide-Area Network for remote operations, requiring at least T1 bandwidth dedicated links.

flowchartDiagram.syntaxExplanation: This flowchart shows the complete observing lifecycle from science program submission through queue scheduling, observation execution, data acquisition, and archival, illustrating the four observing modes and the decision flow for flexible scheduling.
flowchartDiagram.code:
flowchart TD
    SciPlan["Astronomer Creates Science Program"] --> Validate["OCS Validates Program"]
    Validate --> Queue["Add to Observation Queue"]
    Queue --> Scheduler{"Scheduling Mode?"}
    Scheduler -->|Queue-Based| FlexSched["Flexible Scheduler Evaluates"]
    Scheduler -->|Interactive| DirectObs["Direct Interactive Operation"]
    Scheduler -->|Service| ServiceObs["Service Observer Executes"]
    Scheduler -->|Remote| RemoteObs["Remote Observer Connected"]
    FlexSched --> CheckConditions{"Check Conditions"}
    CheckConditions --> Weather["Weather Assessment"]
    CheckConditions --> Targets["Target Positions"]
    CheckConditions --> InstrConfig["Instrument Configuration"]
    Weather --> Resort["Resort Queue - Optimal Program First"]
    Targets --> Resort
    InstrConfig --> Resort
    Resort --> Dispatch["Dispatch to Sequencer"]
    DirectObs --> Dispatch
    ServiceObs --> Dispatch
    RemoteObs --> Dispatch
    Dispatch --> ConfigInstr["Configure Active Instrument"]
    ConfigInstr --> Execute["Execute Observation Sequence"]
    Execute --> DetectorRead["Detector Readout"]
    DetectorRead --> LocalStore["Store Local Copy in IOC"]
    DetectorRead --> SystemDisk["Transfer to System Data Disk"]
    SystemDisk --> QuickLook{"Quick-Look Quality OK?"}
    QuickLook -->|Yes| Archive["Automatic Archival"]
    QuickLook -->|No| AdjustParams["Adjust Parameters"]
    AdjustParams --> Execute
    Archive --> FITSExport["FITS Export to Home Institutes"]
    Archive --> NextObs{"More Observations?"}
    NextObs -->|Yes| Queue
    NextObs -->|No| SessionEnd["Observing Session Complete"]
flowchartDiagram.caption: Gemini 8m Telescope Observing Lifecycle

sequenceDiagram.syntaxExplanation: This sequence diagram shows the interaction between the Observatory Control System, Telescope Control System, Instrument Control System, IOC layer, and Archive during a typical queue-based observation, including command routing, data flow, and safety checks.
sequenceDiagram.code:
sequenceDiagram
    participant Ast as Astronomer
    participant OCS as Observatory Control System
    participant Seq as Sequencer
    participant TCS as Telescope Control System
    participant ICS as Instrument Control System
    participant IOC as IOC Layer via EPICS
    participant DHS as Data Handling System
    participant Arch as Archive

    Ast->>OCS: Submit Science Program
    OCS->>OCS: Validate and Queue Program
    OCS->>Seq: Dispatch Optimal Program
    Seq->>TCS: Slew Telescope via Direct Route
    TCS->>IOC: Send Control Commands
    IOC-->>TCS: ACK within 100-200ms
    TCS-->>Seq: Command Accepted within 2s

    Seq->>ICS: Configure Active Instrument
    ICS->>IOC: Set Instrument Parameters
    IOC-->>ICS: Parameters Configured
    ICS-->>Seq: Instrument Ready

    Seq->>ICS: Begin Exposure
    ICS->>IOC: Start Detector Readout
    IOC->>DHS: Transfer Detector Data
    DHS->>DHS: Store Local Copy for Security
    DHS->>DHS: Apply Loss-less Compression
    DHS-->>OCS: Data Available for Quick-Look

    OCS->>DHS: Request Quick-Look Analysis
    DHS-->>OCS: Quality Assessment Results

    DHS->>Arch: Automatic FITS Archival
    Arch-->>DHS: Archival Confirmed

    Note over OCS,Arch: Status updates within 4s at local stations
    Note over TCS,IOC: Safety monitoring continuous on all subsystems
sequenceDiagram.caption: Queue-Based Observation Command and Data Flow

Link chosen to transfer data between systems.
Desirable hardware specification for installed control systems.
