revisionHistory.description: Software Requirements Specification for the MultiMahjong Project by K-Team, Solid Software Pty Ltd
revisionHistory.author: K-Team: Joanna Araminta (PM), Victor Leung, Joel Brakey, Michael Hart (doc maintainer), Dean Cortinovis, Long Tang. Supervisor: Anthony Senyard

introduction.purpose:
This document formally states the requirements for the **MultiMahjong** product — a single/multi player Mahjong computer game. Requirements are prioritized into three levels: Level 1 (Essential) — minimum requirements for acceptance; Level 2 (Highly Desirable) — likely inclusions, time permitting; Level 3 (Desirable) — considered for future modifications. The document covers functional requirements, non-functional requirements, user interface specifications, documentation requirements, acceptance criteria, and behavioural examples.

The client for this project is Steve Goschnick, Managing Director, Solid Software Pty Ltd., Carlton VIC, Australia.
introduction.documentConventions: Requirements are organized into three priority levels: Level 1 (Essential) represents the minimum set for product acceptance; Level 2 (Highly Desirable) represents likely inclusions time permitting; Level 3 (Desirable) represents future modification candidates. Each requirement is numbered hierarchically (e.g., 4.1.1, 4.2.2.3). The document assumes reader familiarity with Chinese rules of Mahjong as described in Carkner, K.J. 'How to play Mah Jong', 1993, Penguin Books Australia Ltd. Level 1 requirements may be superseded by corresponding Level 2 or 3 requirements during implementation. Data dictionary entries use structured notation with aliases, usage references, and descriptions.
introduction.intendedAudience: The primary audience is the K-Team development team, Steve Goschnick (the client) who must sign off on the requirements, the project supervisor Anthony Senyard, and potential stakeholders at Solid Software Pty Ltd who will commercially distribute the MultiMahjongClient product.
introduction.productScope:
**MultiMahjong** is a product consisting of two programs — a **MultiMahjongServer** and a **MultiMahjongClient**. The Server/Client architecture allows up to 4 players to play Chinese Mahjong against each other over a TCP/IP network. The MultiMahjongClient also supports a stand-alone single player mode against 3 Computer Opponents (COs). The system fills empty player slots with COs to always maintain the required 4 players.

The MultiMahjongClient is a commercial product to be sold to end users. The MultiMahjongServer initially resides on a server owned or operated by the client (Solid Software). The system is built using JDK 1.2 (Java) for cross-platform compatibility across PC (Windows 95/98/NT), Macintosh (OS 8), and Unix (Solaris 2.6/7).
references: Carkner, K.J. 'How to play Mah Jong', 1993, Penguin Books Australia Ltd.

overallDescription.productPerspective: The client (Solid Software) has no existing system for MultiMahjong — this is a new product. The system uses a Server/Client architecture where the MultiMahjongServer resides on a central TCP/IP-connected computer and communicates with multiple MultiMahjongClients. In a single player game, the server is not required and the client intercepts and processes data internally. The server relays data between connected clients, automates game initialization (tile randomization, seating positions), and maintains game information. The system supports up to 10 simultaneous games (40 players).
productFunctions: Create and host multi player Mahjong games with up to 4 human players over TCP/IP.
Support single player mode with 3 Computer Opponents without network connection.
Automatically fill empty player positions with Computer Opponents.
Enforce Chinese rules of Mahjong for all game actions (Chow, Pung, Kong, Mahjong).
Provide real-time game state synchronization between all connected clients.
Flexible scheduling via queue-based observation queue resorting based on conditions.
Maintain a High Scores list on the server.
Generate and display game events (fishing alerts, Mahjong declarations, draws).
Allow users to configure games with customizable score limits and CO settings.
Provide user preferences management (name, icon, sound, score limits).

userClassesAndCharacteristics.characteristics: Anyone who enjoys or is interested in playing Mahjong. Range from users with no prior Mahjong knowledge to experienced players. Must have basic PC/Mac/Unix operation knowledge. No advanced TCP/IP networking knowledge required for multiplayer. The product provides documentation sufficient for learning without external training.

Has general knowledge of a Web Server environment. Knows how to set up and operate a TCP/IP server. Understands basic networking principles and knows the hostname and IP address of the server. Uses the server program to manage game infrastructure.

overallDescription.operatingEnvironment: The system runs on any machine supporting a Java Virtual Machine with JDK 1.2. Supported platforms: PC (Windows 95/98/NT), Macintosh (OS 8), Unix (Solaris 2.6 or 7). Minimum hardware: 100 MHz processor, 32 MB RAM, 10 MB disk, 16-bit video at 800x600, 28.8 Kbps modem or LAN for multiplayer. The MultiMahjongServer resides on a TCP/IP-connected server with a unique IP address.
designAndImplementationConstraints: The system must be written using JDK 1.2 from Sun Microsystems.
Coding standard must follow Sun Microsystems' Java Code Conventions (CodeConventions.pdf).
The GUI must fit within 800x600 pixel resolution.
All graphics must be in 16-bit colour.
Any game of Mahjong requires exactly 4 players; the system must enforce this with COs filling gaps.
The CO must not have access to concealed tile data (fair play constraint).
In single player mode, the MultiMahjongClient must intercept and process data internally without a server.
userDocumentation: Administrator's manual for MultiMahjongServer: installation, setup, background knowledge, TCP/IP configuration, troubleshooting.
User's manual for MultiMahjongClient: installation, game start/join instructions, Mahjong rules and scoring, GUI usage guide, troubleshooting. Available in hard copy and electronic format.
Design documentation: SQAP, SRS, SADD, SDD, SVVP/TP, and source code.
Online help accessible during gameplay (Level 2).
assumptionsAndDependencies: Reader familiarity with Chinese rules of Mahjong as described in the referenced rulebook.
User's computer has been correctly configured for TCP/IP networking (for multiplayer).
JDK 1.2 compatible JVM is available on the target platform.
TCP/IP network connectivity is available for multiplayer games.

externalInterfaceRequirements.userInterfaces:
**MultiMahjongServer**: Level 1 — command-line interface with no GUI. Level 2/3 — optional GUI for administrator to view logs and change settings.

**MultiMahjongClient**: Graphical user interface using mouse and keyboard with keyboard shortcuts. The Main Window displays: current user's tile faces, the wall with remaining tiles, backs of other players' tiles, revealed/exposed tiles from all players, last discarded tile, and action buttons (Pick Up, Discard, Chow, Pung, Kong, Mahjong, Reveal Kong). Inactive buttons are greyed out. A tabbed frame with 3 tabs shows: Tab 1 (Player name, icon, Wind of the Round), Tab 2 (Score, Hand limit), Tab 3 (Preferences).

Dialogue boxes present: game creation/join options, available games list with scroll bar and selectable items, error notifications. Level 3 features include animated buttons when becoming active, animated Chow/Pung/Kong/Mahjong tiles, and a discarded tiles view accessible from the wall frame.
externalInterfaceRequirements.hardwareInterfaces: The software interfaces with standard PC, Macintosh, or Unix hardware through the Java Virtual Machine abstraction layer. Minimum hardware requirements: 100 MHz processor, 32 MB RAM, 10 MB disk, 16-bit video card at 800x600 resolution. Network connectivity requires 28.8 Kbps modem with TCP/IP or LAN connection for multiplayer mode.
externalInterfaceRequirements.softwareInterfaces: The system interfaces with: JDK 1.2 runtime environment, TCP/IP networking stack (provided by the host OS), Java's Unicode standard for potential multi-language support (Level 3). The MultiMahjongClient communicates with the MultiMahjongServer using IP protocol. Game state data, player actions, and initialization information are exchanged between client and server.
externalInterfaceRequirements.communicationsInterfaces: Communication between MultiMahjongClients and the MultiMahjongServer uses TCP/IP networking with IP protocol. The server acts as a relay node, forwarding data from one client to others. In single player mode, no network communication is required — the client processes all data internally. Level 3 includes real-time chat between players via the server.

systemFeatures.name: MultiMahjong Server
systemFeatures.description: Central game server that resides on a TCP/IP-connected computer, manages game sessions, automates initialization, relays data between clients, and maintains persistent game information including a High Scores list. Supports up to 10 simultaneous games. Priority: Essential.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: MultiMahjongClient connects to the server. Response: Server accepts the connection and communicates using IP.
Stimulus: Client sends game creation request with parameters. Response: Server creates new game, randomizes tile order and seating positions, stores game info, and waits for additional players.
Stimulus: All required players have joined and creator starts game. Response: Server sends initialization data (tiles, positions, settings) to all connected clients.
Stimulus: Client sends game action (discard, chow, etc). Response: Server relays the action to all other clients in the game.
functionalRequirements: 4.1.1: The server shall reside on a central computer connected to a TCP/IP network with a unique IP address.
4.1.2: The server shall allow connections from MultiMahjongClients and communicate using IP.
4.1.3: The server shall relay data from one client to other clients that request it.
4.1.4 (L2): The server shall automate game initialization — randomizing tile order, randomizing seating positions, and storing the Game Info database.
4.1.5 (L2): The server shall maintain a High Scores list with names and scores.
4.1.6 (L3): The server shall support real-time chat between players.
4.1.8 (L3): The server shall support hosting all COs centrally instead of on client machines.
4.1.9 (L3): The server shall have a GUI for administrator log access and settings.
4.1.10 (L3): The server shall save/load preferences to/from a file.

systemFeatures.name: Game Initialization
systemFeatures.description: Handles all aspects of creating, joining, and starting Mahjong games for both single and multi player modes. Users configure games by setting names, icons, player counts, CO counts, and score limits. The system retrieves available games from the server for joining. Priority: Essential.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: User launches MultiMahjongClient. Response: Dialogue box presents options to create new multi/single player game, join existing game, or quit.
Stimulus: User creates multi player game with settings. Response: Client sends initialization data to server; server creates game and waits for other players.
Stimulus: User selects join existing game. Response: Client retrieves list of available games from server, displays with creator info, score limits, and available slots.
Stimulus: All players joined and creator starts game. Response: Each client retrieves from server: CO assignment, score limit, all player info, and tile assignments.
functionalRequirements: 4.2.1: Users can create new multi/single player game, join existing game, or quit at any time when no game is active.
4.2.2: Creating a multi player game requires: name, icon selection, number of human/CO opponents (total 4), score limit, and wait for humans to join.
4.2.3: Client must send initialization info to server to create a new game.
4.2.4: CO processing is distributed across clients — in single player, 3 COs on one client; in 2-player, 1 CO each; in 3-player, 1 CO on one client.
4.2.5: Creating single player game requires: name, icon selection, score limit.
4.2.6: Joining retrieves available games list from server.
4.2.7: Joining requires: name, icon selection, and game selection.
4.2.9: Each client retrieves from server: CO support assignment, score limit, all player details, and assigned tiles.
4.2.11 (L2): Client saves/loads user preferences to/from file.
4.2.12 (L3): Game creator can set CO ability level — Beginner, Intermediate, Advanced.
4.2.13 (L3): Game creator can choose rule variations (Cleared Hand, Japanese).
4.2.14 (L3): Users can upload custom JPEG/GIF icons.

systemFeatures.name: Gameplay — Playing the Game
systemFeatures.description: Core game loop implementing Chinese Mahjong rules including tile picking, discarding, set formation (Chow/Pung/Kong), Mahjong declaration, fishing alerts, and round/wind progression. Game state is synchronized across all clients in real time. Priority: Essential.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: It is the user's turn to pick up. Response: Client informs user it is their turn; user picks from wall; action sent to server and relayed to all clients.

Stimulus: Another player discards a tile that allows user to Chow/Pung/Kong/Mahjong. Response: Client detects the possibility, activates corresponding buttons, and waits for user action. If user acts, action is sent to server.
Stimulus: A player goes Mahjong. Response: Server notifies all clients; Wind of the Round updated if required; new hand begins.
Stimulus: Hand ends in a draw. Response: All clients informed; hand is replayed.
functionalRequirements: 4.3.1: Client must enforce Chinese Mahjong rules for all moves.
4.3.2: Client informs user whose turn it is to pick up.
4.3.3: Client retrieves and displays other players' actions: wall pickup, exposed/revealed sets, and discards.
4.3.4: Client detects and informs user of available Chow/Pung/Kong/Mahjong opportunities and sends the action to server if chosen.
4.3.5: Users can pick up tiles from the wall on their turn.
4.3.6: Users can discard a tile after picking up.
4.3.7: Client informs user when another player is fishing.
4.3.8: Client informs user when another player goes Mahjong and updates Wind of the Round.
4.3.9: Client informs user when a hand ends in a draw.
4.3.10 (L2): Client keeps user's tiles sorted in order.
4.3.11 (L2): Client plays sound effects for game actions.
4.3.15 (L2): Client automates wall pickup when user has no other option.
4.3.16 (L3): Undo last move in single player mode.
4.3.17 (L3): Save game in single player mode.
4.3.18 (L3): Real-time chat between players.

systemFeatures.name: Game Ending
systemFeatures.description: Handles game termination conditions including natural completion (4 rounds), user-initiated exit, and premature player departure with CO replacement. Priority: Essential.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: All 4 rounds completed. Response: Game ends; users presented with options to quit or start/join new game.
Stimulus: User chooses to end game early. Response: User exits; presented with quit or new game options.
Stimulus: A player leaves mid-game. Response: Server informs all clients; a CO is created on another user's machine to take over the departing player's position and tiles.
functionalRequirements: 4.4.1: Game ends after all 4 rounds or when user chooses to end.
4.4.2: User can end game at any stage, then quit or begin/join a new game.
4.4.3: Server informs all connected clients when game ends; users get quit or new game options.
4.4.4 (L2): If a user leaves prematurely, a CO is created on another machine to fill their position.
4.4.5 (L3): Game creator decides the CO ability level for replacement COs.

systemFeatures.name: Computer Opponent (CO)
systemFeatures.description: AI opponent that plays Mahjong following Chinese rules with fair-play constraints (no access to concealed information). Supports multiple ability levels with increasing strategic sophistication from simple rule-following to look-ahead algorithms. Priority: Essential.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: CO's turn to act. Response: CO evaluates available tiles, applies strategy algorithms, and makes a move within 1 minute.
Stimulus: Another player discards a tile. Response: CO checks if it can Chow/Pung/Kong/Mahjong and acts if strategically appropriate.
functionalRequirements: 4.5.1: CO must play moves according to Chinese Mahjong rules.
4.5.2: CO must not access concealed tile data from the wall or other players' hands.
4.5.3: CO can access the count of tiles in the wall or any player's hand.
4.5.4: CO can read any player's exposed or revealed tiles.
4.5.5: CO can pick up tiles, discard, reveal Kongs, and Chow/Pung/Kong/Mahjong.
4.5.6 (L2): CO can determine unplayed tiles based on visible discards, exposures, and revelations.
4.5.7 (L2): CO recognizes 'almost finished' sets and avoids discarding tiles in such sets (basic strategy).
4.5.8 (L3): CO supports 3 ability levels — Beginner, Intermediate, Advanced.
4.5.9 (L3): CO performs look-ahead algorithms for best move determination (Intermediate/Advanced).
4.5.10 (L3): CO can perform calculations at any stage of the game.
4.5.11 (L3): CO can play any supported rule variation.
4.5.12 (L3): Multiple CO playing styles available for selection.

performanceRequirements: 5.5.1: CO must respond within 1 minute in single and multi player modes.
5.5.2: Server must handle up to 10 simultaneous games (max 40 players).
5.5.3: Client must calculate possible Chow/Pung/Kong/Mahjong moves within 5 seconds of last discard.
safetyRequirements: Not applicable — the system is a recreational game with no safety-critical operations.
securityRequirements: Data exchanged between machines does not contain private information and is not capable of undermining system integrity. Encryption and data protection are not needed.
softwareQualityAttributes: Cross-platform portability: Must run on any JDK 1.2 compatible JVM (Windows 95/98/NT, Mac OS 8, Solaris 2.6/7).
Fair play: CO must not access concealed tile data.
Usability: Users with no Mahjong knowledge can learn from included documentation; no advanced networking knowledge required.
Error recovery: Fatal errors terminate cleanly; nonfatal errors display dialogue and allow continued play.
Internationalization (L3): Unicode support for multiple languages via Java's Unicode standard.
businessRules: The MultiMahjongClient is a commercial product for sale to end users.
The MultiMahjongServer initially resides on client-operated infrastructure.
Level 1 requirements define minimum acceptance criteria.
Level 1 requirements may be superseded by corresponding Level 2/3 implementations.

otherRequirements: 5.2.1.1: Nonfatal errors display a dialogue box reporting the error nature; user can continue.
5.2.2.1: Fatal errors display a dialogue box; user must quit; all processes cleaned up.
5.2.1.3 (L2): Error dialogue includes option to access online troubleshooting guide.
7.3.1: All design and coding documentation delivered to client.
7.3.2: Documentation available electronically; non-source documents in hard copy.

glossary.definition: A set of 3 sequentially numbered tiles from a given suit.
A Kong, Pung, or Chow formed entirely by picking up from The Wall.
Computer Opponent — the entity that calculates and executes the computer's moves.
All tiles which have already been discarded.
Tiles at the end of The Wall used to replenish the extra tile used in getting a Kong.
A small window expecting input data from the user, usually containing text and buttons.
The tile a player chooses to discard at the end of their turn.
Red, Green, and White Dragon Tiles — 4 of each (12 total).
Occurs when 3 players go Mahjong simultaneously or all tiles up to the dead wall are drawn without any player achieving Mahjong.
When a discard is picked up to form a Kong, Pung, or Chow — must be shown to all players for the rest of the hand.
When a player needs only 1 tile to go Mahjong — all other players must be alerted.
Ends when 4 rounds have been played.
The tiles each player has. A winning hand is when a player goes Mahjong by forming an ordinary or special hand.
Special tiles in Mahjong — either Dragon Tiles or Wind Tiles.
A set of 4 identical tiles.
The highest amount that can be scored for a winning hand.
A 4-player game originating in China c. 500 BC. Also the term for a winning hand.
A hand containing any four sets of Kongs, Pungs, and Chows, plus a pair.
2 of the same tile.
A set of 3 identical tiles.
When a player reveals a concealed Kong to avoid being a tile short for Mahjong.
Completed when the player who was the original Wind of the Round is that wind again.
An exact arrangement of designated tiles constituting a winning hand.
Three suits: Bamboos, Characters, Circles. Each has tiles numbered 1-9 with 4 of each (108 total).
Transmission Control Protocol/Internet Protocol — communications protocols for linking dissimilar computers across networks.
A 4-sided arrangement of tiles, 17 tiles long and 2 tiles high, from which each hand is played.
Playing pieces in Mahjong (like cards in Gin) — either Suit Tiles or Honour Tiles.
Player position — East, South, West, or North.
The wind whose round it currently is. Important for scoring and game completion. Rotates: East, South, West, North.
East, South, West, and North Wind Tiles — 4 of each (16 total).

flowchartDiagram.syntaxExplanation: This flowchart shows the complete game session lifecycle from client launch through game setup, gameplay, and game ending.
flowchartDiagram.code:
flowchart TD
    Launch["Launch MultiMahjongClient"] --> Options{"Choose Option"}
    Options -->|Create Multi| CreateMulti["Enter Name, Icon, Players, Limit"]
    Options -->|Create Single| CreateSingle["Enter Name, Icon, Limit"]
    Options -->|Join Existing| FetchList["Fetch Available Games List"]
    Options -->|Quit| Exit["Exit Program"]
    CreateMulti --> SendToServer["Send Init Data to Server"]
    SendToServer --> WaitPlayers["Wait for Human Players"]
    WaitPlayers --> StartGame{"All Players Joined?"}
    StartGame -->|Yes| Initialize["Server Randomizes Tiles and Seats"]
    StartGame -->|No| WaitPlayers
    CreateSingle --> InitLocal["Initialize Locally with 3 COs"]
    FetchList --> SelectGame["Select Game from List"]
    SelectGame --> JoinGame["Send Join Info to Server"]
    JoinGame --> Initialize
    InitLocal --> GameLoop
    Initialize --> DistributeData["Distribute Tiles and Settings"]
    DistributeData --> GameLoop{"Game Loop"}
    GameLoop --> PickUp["Current Player Picks Up Tile"]
    PickUp --> CheckActions{"Can Chow/Pung/Kong/Mahjong?"}
    CheckActions -->|Yes| ActionChoice{"Player Acts?"}
    CheckActions -->|No| DiscardTile["Discard a Tile"]
    ActionChoice -->|Yes| PerformAction["Execute Action"]
    ActionChoice -->|No| DiscardTile
    PerformAction --> CheckMahjong{"Mahjong?"}
    DiscardTile --> NextPlayer["Next Player's Turn"]
    NextPlayer --> GameLoop
    CheckMahjong -->|Yes| UpdateWind["Update Wind of Round"]
    CheckMahjong -->|No| NextPlayer
    UpdateWind --> CheckRounds{"4 Rounds Complete?"}
    CheckRounds -->|Yes| GameEnd["Game Over"]
    CheckRounds -->|No| NewHand["Deal New Hand"]
    NewHand --> GameLoop
    GameEnd --> Options
flowchartDiagram.caption: MultiMahjong Game Session Lifecycle

sequenceDiagram.syntaxExplanation: This sequence diagram shows the interaction between two human players, the server, and a computer opponent during a multi player game session.
sequenceDiagram.code:
sequenceDiagram
    participant Bob as Bob's Client
    participant Server as MultiMahjongServer
    participant Sally as Sally's Client
    participant CO as Computer Opponent

    Bob->>Server: Create Game (name, icon, 2 humans, 2 COs, limit)
    Server->>Server: Store Game Info
    Sally->>Server: Request Available Games
    Server-->>Sally: Games List (Bob's game - 1 slot open)
    Sally->>Server: Join Bob's Game (name, icon)
    Server-->>Bob: Sally Joined Notification
    Bob->>Server: Start Game Command
    Server->>Server: Randomize Tiles and Seats
    Server-->>Bob: Init Data (tiles, CO assignment, positions)
    Server-->>Sally: Init Data (tiles, CO assignment, positions)
    Note over Bob,CO: Bob's client manages CO1, Sally's manages CO2

    Bob->>Server: Pick Up Tile from Wall
    Server-->>Sally: Bob Picked Up
    Bob->>Server: Discard Tile
    Server-->>Sally: Bob Discarded [tile]
    Sally->>Sally: Check Chow/Pung/Kong/Mahjong
    Sally->>Server: Chow Action
    Server-->>Bob: Sally Chow'd
    CO->>CO: Evaluate Best Move (within 1 min)
    CO->>Server: CO Discards Tile
    Server-->>Bob: CO Discarded [tile]
    Server-->>Sally: CO Discarded [tile]

    Sally->>Server: Mahjong Declaration
    Server-->>Bob: Sally goes Mahjong
    Server->>Server: Update Wind of Round
    Note over Server: New hand begins
sequenceDiagram.caption: Multi Player Game Session with Server Relay

dataDictionary.name: player action
dataDictionary.description: [mouse state | keystroke]; mouse state = coordinate + button state; coordinate = xval + yval (pixels).

dataDictionary.name: pref. data
dataDictionary.description: [current player's name (30 char unicode) | icon (JPEG/GIF) | limit for winning hand (6 digit) | sound on/off (boolean)].

dataDictionary.name: screen changes
dataDictionary.description: Commands: display preferences window, update icon, update name, update game area, display action button, updated score.

dataDictionary.name: game data
dataDictionary.description: [tile position | current round | first Wind of Round | current Wind of Round | current player]; tile position = [Wall | Discard | Dead Tile | Exposed Set | Revealed Kong].

dataDictionary.name: game action
dataDictionary.description: [discarded tile | picked up tile | exposed tile].

revisionHistory.description: System/Subsystem Specification (SSS) for the Tactical Control System (TCS) Block 0 — identifies, specifies, and establishes detailed system requirements for UAV command and control
revisionHistory.author: Naval Surface Warfare Center-Dahlgren Division (NSWCDD) and Joint Technology Center/System Integration Laboratory (JTC/SIL), U.S. Army Missile Command, Redstone Arsenal, AL. Prepared for: Program Executive Officer, Cruise Missiles Project and Unmanned Air Vehicles Joint Project

introduction.purpose: This document identifies, specifies, and establishes the detailed system requirements for the **Tactical Control System (TCS)** as set forth by the Operational Requirements Document (ORD) for the Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (UAV) Tactical Control System, Version 5.0. The TCS is a Department of Defense (DoD) system that provides the military services with a single command, control, data receipt, data processing, data export and dissemination system interoperable with all present and future tactical unmanned aerial vehicles. The SSS specifies methods to ensure each requirement is met. External interface requirements are covered in separate Interface Design Descriptions (IDDs). Published IAW DID DI-IPSC-81431.
introduction.documentConventions: Requirements are identified by unique SSS identifiers (e.g., [SSS004], [SSS010]) traced to Operational Requirements Document (ORD) entries. The document follows MIL-STD-498 format. Five discrete levels of UAV interaction are defined (Level 1–5). Requirements marked **DELETED** have been removed from the specification. All requirements are of equal weight and criticality unless identified in the traceability table. Precedence: TCS ORD > TCS SSS > Other TCS requirement documents > Referenced documents.
introduction.intendedAudience: U.S. military services (Army, Navy, Air Force, Marine Corps), UAV Joint Project Office (JPO), Naval Surface Warfare Center - Dahlgren Division, Joint Technology Center/SIL, TCS Program Manager, integrated product teams (IPTs) for Software, System Engineering, C4I, Data Links, Training, Logistics, and System Test, and defense contractors involved in TCS development.
introduction.productScope:
The **TCS** provides command, control, data receipt, processing, export and dissemination for tactical UAVs including the **Outrider** (TUAV) and **Predator** (MAE UAV), their payloads, and other network communication systems. TCS also receives and processes information from High Altitude Endurance (HAE) UAVs, future development UAVs and payloads.

TCS consists of software, software-related hardware, and ground support hardware. It provides connectivity to 21+ C4I systems. TCS is DII/COE compliant, non-proprietary, and the architectural standard for all future tactical UAVs. Software is the major focus — providing tools for communications, mission tasking, mission planning, mission execution, data receipt, processing, limited exploitation, and dissemination.

Design and development conducted in two phases: Phase 1 (Program Definition and Risk Reduction, 24 months) and Phase 2 (Engineering and Manufacturing Development with Low Rate Initial Production).
references: MIL-STD-2500A National Imagery Transmission Format (Version 2.0), 18 June 1993
MIL-STD-498 Software Development and Documentation, 5 December 1994
ORD for UAV Tactical Control System (Version 5.0)
ORD for Tactical Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (TUAV), October 1996
ORD for Medium Altitude Endurance (MAE) UAV
Operational Concept Document for TCS, 18 February 1997
MIL-STD-1472 Human Engineering Design Criteria for Military Systems
MIL-STD-882 Safety
UAV TCS Program Management Plan (Version 1), 19 February 1997
JROC Memorandum
TCS to C4I Interface Design Descriptions (21+ IDDs: TCS 200-256)

overallDescription.productPerspective: The TCS is a new system developed by the UAV Joint Project Office to provide a single, unified control system for multiple types of tactical UAVs. It replaces the need for separate ground control stations for each UAV type. TCS operates within the DoD tactical information infrastructure, interfacing with 21+ C4I systems including ASAS, JSTARS GSM, JMCIS, JSIPS, AFATDS, ATWCS, CARS, ETRAC, IAS, ATHS, COMPASS, TBMCS, TROJAN SPIRIT II, and service-specific mission planners (AMPS, AFMSS, TAMPS). The system is designed for land-based shelters, shipboard, submarine, and airborne platforms. TCS complies with DII/COE, COSIP, CIGSS, VMF/JMF, NITF 2.0, and JTA standards.
productFunctions: Mission Planning: Generate, receive, process, transmit mission plans including flight route plans (500 waypoints), payload plans, and communications plans with interactive map-based GUI
Mission Control and Monitoring: Control and monitor air vehicles (flight, navigation, launch/recovery), payloads (EO/IR/SAR), and data terminals (LOS and SATCOM) during mission execution
Payload Product Management: Receive, process, display, and exploit payload data from EO, IR, and SAR sensors with NITF 2.0 compliance
Targeting: Determine target coordinates, estimate target accuracy, support target location functions
C4I System Interface: Interface with 21+ DoD C4I systems for tasking, data dissemination, and tactical communications
Maintenance: Execute AV, payload, data terminal, and workstation maintenance software with FD/L diagnostics
Software Upgrade and Debug: Install upgrades via CD-ROM with password protection, import DTED/DFAD/ADRG map data

userClassesAndCharacteristics.characteristics: Military personnel trained on TCS to control different UAV types and payloads. Operates in cold weather clothing and Mission Oriented Protective Posture (MOPP). Requires high-resolution GUI with X-windows/Motif. Cleared to highest data sensitivity level. Trained via embedded interactive courseware.
Performs mission control, monitoring, updates and modifications using ergonomically designed controls (5th percentile female to 95th percentile male). Uses trackball, mouse, joystick, and keyboard.

Authorized operator who installs software upgrades, modifies programmable parameters, and executes debug/diagnostic capabilities. Password-protected access. Maintains system per Services' approved UAV maintenance concepts.
Program Executive Officer, IPT leads, and QA teams responsible for oversight, verification, and acceptance of TCS requirements across development phases.

overallDescription.operatingEnvironment: TCS operates in land-based shelter and shipboard environments as specified in the SSDD. Hardware is ruggedized for inter and intra theater movement. Operates with worldwide 110/220V 50/60Hz power. Deployable by ground, air (C-130, CH-47/CH-53D), sea, and rail. Configurable for transport in 2 hours or less. Capable of continuous operation for 72 hours minimum. Monitors readable under direct sunlight and low light levels. Compatible with MOPP conditions.
designAndImplementationConstraints: DII/COE compliant software architecture, non-proprietary with unlimited data rights
Software developed per tailored MIL-STD-498
Modular architecture supporting future UAVs, payloads, and capabilities
NDI/COTS hardware prioritized; open architecture standards for all hardware selection
Year 2000 compliant for all HWCIs and CSCIs [SSS567]
FIPS 151-2 (POSIX.1) compliant [SSS381]
X-windows and Motif based HCI [SSS447]
Throughput not to exceed 50% (objective 25%) over any 10-second period
50% spare memory storage capacity (objective 75%)
No single hardware failure or software error shall result in unsafe AV command
Must comply with MIL-STD-882C for safety
userDocumentation: TCS System/Subsystem Specification (SSS)
System/Subsystem Design Document (SSDD)
Software Requirements Specification (SRS) per CSCI
Software Design Document (SDD) per CSCI
Hardware Design Document (HDD)
Interface Design Documents (IDDs) for all interfaces
Version Description Documents (VDD)
Operator Manuals and Technical Manuals (verified and validated prior to initial operational test)
Embedded interactive training courseware
assumptionsAndDependencies: Predator and Outrider UAV programs provide their respective AVs, payloads, and data terminals
C4I systems are operational and interfaces defined per individual IDDs
DoD tactical radio infrastructure (UHF, VHF, HF) is available for external communications
NIMA mapping data (DTED, DFAD, ADRG, CADRG) is available via CD-ROM
Government will complete radar cross section analysis for threat lookup tables
Phase 2 design/construction specifications will be revised for formal military standards

externalInterfaceRequirements.userInterfaces: High-resolution, computer-generated graphical user interface based on X-windows and Motif (DII Style Manager). Each console displays minimum 4 windows: (1) map with AV position, flight path, waypoints; (2) AV flight/payload data with downlinked video; (3) telemetry data graphics; (4) workstation software menus. Window-in-window format supported. Controls: keyboard, trackball, mouse, joystick with full keyset/pointing device redundancy. Ergonomic design for 5th percentile female to 95th percentile male. Visual alerts with priority display; auditory alerts adjustable to 20dB above speech interference level. Color-coded, flashing warning messages per MIL-STD-1472. Maximum 1-second delay from operator command to system response.
externalInterfaceRequirements.hardwareInterfaces: Interfaces with: data terminals (LOS and SATCOM simultaneously), external printers, external data storage systems, DoD standard power supply (110/220V 50/60Hz), geopositional data sources (GPS), uninterruptible power supply. Internal interfaces to: AV Standard Interface, Real Time Processor, SAR Processor, intercom, VCR, CD-ROM drive, tape drive, RAID storage.
externalInterfaceRequirements.softwareInterfaces: DII/COE compliant with UCP/CMP for TACCOM messaging (USMTF, ATCSS, FATDS formats). NITF 2.0 for imagery. VMF and JMF for variable/joint message formats. CIGSS and USIS compliant imagery processing. Open software architecture supporting additional CSCIs, CSCs, CSUs. Non-proprietary with unlimited data rights. Hosted on variety of service-specific operating systems.
externalInterfaceRequirements.communicationsInterfaces: External: TCP/IP, RS-232, RS-422, RS-170A video, 2-wire/4-wire wireline, MSE (DNVT/DSVT), SINCGARS, ANG/GYC-7, IEEE 802.3/LAN. Data links: LOS and SATCOM (simultaneous support). Tactical radios: UHF, VHF, UHF/VHF, HF for digital message and voice. C4I integration via DII/COE UCP/CMP. 21+ C4I system interfaces with phased implementation schedule (FY97–FY01).

systemFeatures.name: Mission Planning Function
systemFeatures.description: Comprehensive UAV mission planning capability including flight route planning with up to 500 waypoints, payload planning, and communications planning. Features interactive map-based GUI with threat overlays, fuel calculations, terrain avoidance warnings, and mission plan validation. Supports plan exchange with other TCSs and service-specific mission planners. Priority: Essential.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: Operator requests new mission plan. Response: TCS presents map display with planning tools; operator defines waypoints, payload settings, data link parameters, and LOL plan.

Stimulus: Operator completes mission plan and requests validation. Response: TCS checks altitude, payload, data link range, airspace, fuel, threat, terrain masking, and LOL constraints; reports discrepancies or successful completion.
Stimulus: Operator uploads mission plan to AV. Response: TCS transmits flight route and payload plans to AV via selected data link or direct ground connection.
Stimulus: Mission plan received from C4I system. Response: TCS automatically processes and extracts mission planning data.
functionalRequirements: SSS050: TCS shall generate UAV mission plans
SSS053: TCS shall receive and process mission plans from service-specific systems (TAMPS, AMPS, AFMSS)
SSS054: TCS shall receive and process mission plans from other TCSs
SSS057: Mission Plan includes Flight Route Plan, Payload Plan, and Communications Plan
SSS058: Store minimum 500 mission plans under unique names
SSS059: Graphical user interface with map-based waypoint definition using pointing device with keyset redundancy
SSS073: Automatically validate mission plan prior to upload (altitude, payload, data link, airspace, fuel, threat, LOL)
SSS080: Support up to 500 waypoints per flight route plan
SSS083-087: Flight planning tools (weight/balance, fuel, terrain avoidance, data link altitude, payload search area, flight corridors)
SSS088: Display estimated time of arrival and fuel status at each waypoint
SSS076: Compute AV flight range based on remaining fuel at every waypoint and every 5 minutes
SSS090: Flight planning calculations within 10% accuracy vs validated 6-DOF simulator
SSS095: Map display with zoom, scroll, print, clear, and scale indication
SSS096-101: Map icons for AV position, payload FOV, targets, TCS/GDT position, launch/recovery sites, waypoints

systemFeatures.name: Mission Control and Monitoring
systemFeatures.description: Real-time command and control of air vehicles, payloads, and data terminals during mission execution. Supports simultaneous control of two MAE AVs beyond line of sight. Includes AV flight control, navigation, ADT control, launch/recovery, payload control, and data terminal management across all 5 interaction levels. Priority: Essential.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: Operator selects specific AV for control. Response: TCS initializes AV-specific components, establishes data link, displays AV telemetry and status.
Stimulus: AV deviates more than 10% from projected flight path. Response: TCS detects deviation and notifies operator.
Stimulus: Loss of Link detected. Response: TCS displays LOL timer, computes estimated AV position based on last known position and flight mode, attempts to reestablish communications.
Stimulus: Operator requests payload control change. Response: TCS sends payload commands via uplink to AV; displays updated payload status.
functionalRequirements: SSS011: Interoperable with Predator and Outrider across 5 interaction levels
SSS108: Full control of two MAE AVs simultaneously BLOS
SSS112: Control and monitor multiple AV types
SSS113: Notify operator when AV parameters out of limits
SSS114-115: Pass/receive AV control to/from another TCS
SSS118: Control AV flight commands and release to autonomous mode
SSS124: Control AV via LOS and SATCOM data links
SSS128-131: Command flight behavior characteristics and navigation methods with manual override
SSS132-134: ADT control including antenna pointing, transmitter/receiver power and frequencies
SSS135-138: Support automatic launch/recovery (IBLS, CARS) as objective
SSS139: Emergency recovery feature initiation
SSS140-142: Monitor telemetry real-time, detect 10% flight path deviations, monitor all AV subsystems
SSS143-146: Display AV status, last known values during link interruption, fuel parameters, estimated position during LOL
SSS147-155: Payload control and monitoring with graphical interface and automated/preprogrammed override
SSS158-180: Data terminal control (LOS and SATCOM simultaneously) with antenna pointing, transmitter/receiver control and monitoring

systemFeatures.name: Payload Product Management
systemFeatures.description: Processing, display, and limited exploitation of payload sensor data from EO, IR, and SAR payloads. Includes NITF 2.0 compliance, 24-hour data storage, image enhancement, annotation, frozen-frame capture, and multi-payload simultaneous viewing. Priority: Essential.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: Payload data received via downlink. Response: TCS processes, formats, stores, and routes video; generates NITF 2.0 files with telemetry for C4I export.
Stimulus: Operator requests image exploitation. Response: TCS provides contrast, brightness, edge enhancement, sharpness controls; allows annotation and frozen-frame capture.
Stimulus: Operator requests payload product export. Response: TCS formats digital imagery and tactical messages for transmission to C4I systems.
functionalRequirements: SSS182: Process EO, IR, and SAR payload data
SSS184: Store up to 24 hours of payload data (external storage permitted)
SSS185: Comply with CIGSS, USIS, NITF 2.0, and GCCS standards
SSS186: NITF 2.0 files contain telemetry and support data for C4I exploitation
SSS187: Built-in text annotation on imagery
SSS188: Receive secondary HAE UAV payload imagery
SSS190-194: Format, store, route, record video; create/store freeze frames; retrieve/display video; print freeze frames; process digital imagery for export
SSS195-198: Display live/recorded imagery with annotation/overlay; selectable crosshairs; RS170A video routing
SSS200-203: Limited exploitation with image enhancement (contrast, brightness, edge, sharpness); voice/textual reporting; NRT imagery display with date/time, coordinates, north arrow, AV position

systemFeatures.name: C4I System Interface
systemFeatures.description: Comprehensive interface to 21+ DoD C4I systems for tactical communications, data dissemination, and mission coordination. Supports digital and analog interfaces with USMTF, TACFIRE, OTH-Gold, and IEWCOMCAT message formats. Priority: Essential.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: Tasking order received from C4I system. Response: TCS receives, processes, displays tasking; auto-checks for errors.
Stimulus: Operator prepares imagery for dissemination. Response: TCS formats NITF 2.0 files and RS-170A video with/without overlay for transmission to selected C4I systems.
Stimulus: Tactical message received. Response: TCS validates, logs, and presents to operator; flags uncorrectable errors.
functionalRequirements: SSS209: Interface with DII-COE compliant C4I networks (21+ systems)
SSS210: Manage all C4I interfaces for text messages, NITF 2.0, and RS-170A video
SSS211: Support tasking, payload product presentation, and UAV data export
SSS222-228: Digital and analog interface control (send/receive messages, imagery; establish/terminate communications)
SSS230-238: Digital data processing (create USMTF/TACFIRE/OTH-Gold messages; review incoming messages; prepare/review digital imagery)
SSS234-236: Auto-error-checking, flag uncorrectable messages, log all messages
SSS239-244: Monitor all C4I interface status; display online systems; monitor/review all tactical messages and imagery
SSS285: Interface with DoD tactical radios (UHF, VHF, HF), MSE, and satellite communications
SSS290: Utilize DII/COE UCP/CMP for TACCOM messaging

systemFeatures.name: Maintenance and Fault Detection
systemFeatures.description: Software-driven maintenance and diagnostics for all TCS components including AV, payload, data terminal, workstation, and peripheral equipment. Provides Startup, Periodic, and Extensive Fault Detection/Location (FD/L) to Line Replaceable Unit (LRU) level. Priority: Essential.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: TCS enters Maintenance Mode. Response: System enables concurrent maintenance functions for AV, payload, data terminal, and workstation.
Stimulus: Startup FD/L initiated. Response: TCS diagnoses operator workstation readiness.
Stimulus: Authorized operator requests software upgrade. Response: TCS validates password, installs from CD-ROM; restricts unauthorized access.
functionalRequirements: SSS245-248: Execute maintenance software and display results for AV, payload, data terminal, workstation
SSS249-253: FD/L to LRU level; startup FD/L for workstation; operator control of AV/payload/data link FD/L
SSS276-279: Software upgrade via CD-ROM with password protection; modify programmable parameters
SSS280-281: Import NIMA DTED, DFAD, ADRG maps via CD-ROM; incorporate vector and CADRG formats
SSS282-283: Software debug capability with password-protected access

systemFeatures.name: States and Modes Management
systemFeatures.description: TCS operates in three non-concurrent states (Startup, Operations, Shutdown) with multiple modes. Startup includes Normal (60-second initialization) and Recovery (45-second resume). Operations includes Normal, Training, and Maintenance modes. Shutdown stores/deletes mission data and terminates interfaces. Priority: Essential.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: Power applied to TCS. Response: System determines previous shutdown mode and enters Normal or Recovery Startup accordingly; determines achievable interaction levels.
Stimulus: Operator selects Operations Mode. Response: TCS enters selected mode (Normal/Training/Maintenance) with concurrent function support.

Stimulus: Abnormal termination occurs. Response: On next power-up, TCS enters Recovery Startup; prompts operator to (1) resume same modes/data, (2) resume same modes with data review, or (3) perform command shutdown and normal restart.
functionalRequirements: SSS014-015: Three non-concurrent states: Startup, Operations, Shutdown
SSS016-020: Startup state enters on power; mode determined by previous shutdown type
SSS024: Normal Startup initializes within 60 seconds
SSS029: Recovery Startup resumes within 45 seconds
SSS031: Recovery prompts for resume/review/restart options
SSS032-033: Operations state has three non-concurrent modes
SSS037-042: Normal, Training, and Maintenance modes support concurrent functions per interaction level
SSS043-048: Shutdown stores/deletes data, terminates functions and interfaces; no modes in shutdown
SSS528: Automatically record system state data for event reconstruction

performanceRequirements: SSS409: Data latency not greater than Predator or Outrider GCS (whichever is smaller)
SSS441: Plan and launch mission within 1 hour of tasking after emplacement
SSS442: Continuous operation for minimum 72 hours
SSS559: Maximum 1-second delay from operator command to system response
SSS389-392: Throughput not to exceed 50% (objective 25%) over 10-second periods; 50% spare memory (objective 75%)
SSS414-416: 50% unused memory, 50% unused throughput over 10s (20% over 1s), 50% I/O channel reserve
safetyRequirements: SSS345-346: All safety requirements per MIL-STD-882C except nuclear safety
SSS347-352: AV safety — instrument flight cues, certified envelope, cautions/warnings, terrain avoidance, FAA separation, malfunction alerts
SSS353: No single hardware failure shall produce unsafe AV command
SSS556: No single software error shall produce unsafe AV command
SSS354: Restore power to avoid loss of AV control during outages
SSS355-356: Monitor all uplinks/downlinks; reestablish communications on LOL
SSS357-358: Human safety per MIL-STD-2036 and MIL-STD-1472D
securityRequirements: SSS361: AIS security per DoD Directive 5200.28(D)
SSS362: Accredited by Designated Approving Authority before processing classified data
SSS363-364: Risk-based computer security with data sensitivity determined by interfacing systems
SSS365: Communications security per DoD Directive C-5200.5
SSS366-367: Physical security against EW threats and unauthorized disclosure/destruction
SSS368: Approved for operation at interface system level
SSS369-371: Personnel cleared to highest data sensitivity; local site security procedures; security training program
softwareQualityAttributes: SSS410: Reliability MTBF equal to or greater than Predator/Outrider ORD specifications
SSS411-412: Maintainability MTTR per ORDs; minimize maintenance frequency, simplify maintenance, minimize special tools, improve BITE
SSS413: Availability (Ao) per formula: (OT+ST)/(OT+ST+TPM+TCM+TALDT) meeting ORD specifications
SSS417-420: Flexibility via DII/COE and open architecture; portability across hardware platforms and operating systems
SSS421-424: Testability with functional/physical partitioning, fault isolation, and defined test points
SSS425: Common software architecture for Predator, Outrider, and future tactical UAVs
businessRules: All requirements of equal weight unless noted in traceability matrix
All TCS HWCIs and CSCIs must be Year 2000 compliant
NDI selection prioritized, then COTS; non-proprietary with unlimited data rights
Phase 1 uses commercial best practices; Phase 2 uses government-approved specifications
Individual armed services identify computing hardware, desired TCS functionality, C4I connectivity, and AV/payload types per deployment concept

otherRequirements: SSS432-436: Electromagnetic radiation control, EMI minimization, compatibility with operational environment

SSS443-490: Human Factors Engineering — ergonomic controls, MOPP-compatible, sunlight-readable displays, color-coded warnings, visual/auditory alerts, window-in-window format, automated guidance for crisis situations
SSS491-503: Training — embedded interactive courseware, self-paced instruction, UAV flight simulation, operator/maintainer action recording for assessment, measurable proficiency parameters
SSS504-526: Logistics — per ILSP and service maintenance policies, standard DoD tools/TMDE, ground/air/sea/rail transportable, 2-hour configure/de-configure for transport
SSS531-534: Integration capability for aircraft, submarines, land platforms, and ships

glossary.definition: Tactical Control System — single command, control, data receipt, processing, export and dissemination system for tactical UAVs
Unmanned Aerial Vehicle
Tactical Unmanned Aerial Vehicle (Outrider)
Medium Altitude and Endurance UAV (Predator)
High Altitude and Endurance UAV
Air Vehicle — the unmanned aerial vehicle platform
Command, Control, Communications, Computers, and Intelligence
Defense Information Infrastructure / Common Operating Environment
Air Data Terminal — onboard UAV data link terminal
Ground Data Terminal — ground-based data link terminal
Line of Sight — direct data link communication mode
Satellite Communication — beyond line of sight data link mode
Loss of Link — unplanned interruption of data link between TCS and AV
Beyond Line of Sight — communication mode using satellite relay
Electro-Optic — payload sensor type for visual spectrum imagery
Infrared — payload sensor type for thermal imagery
Synthetic Aperture Radar — payload sensor type for radar imagery
National Imagery Transmission Format — standard format for imagery files (Version 2.0)
Fault Detection/Location — diagnostic capability to identify and locate system faults to LRU level
Line Replaceable Unit — lowest level modular component replaceable in the field
Human-Computer Interface — graphical user interface for operator interaction
Computer Software Configuration Item — identifiable software component
Hardware Configuration Item — identifiable hardware component
Non-Developmental Item — off-the-shelf equipment previously approved for service use
Commercial-Off-The-Shelf — commercially available hardware/software
Mean Time Between Failure — measure of system reliability
Mean Time To Repair — measure of system maintainability
Variable Message Format — DoD tactical message standard
Joint Message Format — DoD joint tactical message standard
Mission Oriented Protective Posture — chemical/biological protective clothing/equipment levels
Digital Terrain Elevation Data — terrain elevation database
Digital Feature Analysis Data — terrain feature database
Arc Digitized Raster Graphic — map format
Common Imagery Ground/Surface System — imagery processing standard
Computer Open Systems Interface Processor — computing standard
Joint Technical Architecture — DoD computing/communications standard

flowchartDiagram.syntaxExplanation: This flowchart shows the TCS operational lifecycle from power-on through the three states (Startup, Operations, Shutdown) and the modes within each state.
flowchartDiagram.code:
flowchart TD
    PowerOn["Power Applied"] --> StartupState{"Startup State"}
    StartupState -->|Normal termination| NormalStartup["Normal Startup Mode\n60-second initialization"]
    StartupState -->|Abnormal termination| RecoveryStartup["Recovery Startup Mode\n45-second resume"]
    NormalStartup --> InitHW["Initialize HWCIs, Download SW,\nStart CSCIs, Execute FD/L,\nEstablish Interface Readiness"]
    RecoveryStartup --> RecoveryOptions{"Recovery Options"}
    RecoveryOptions -->|1| ResumeAll["Resume Same Modes and Data"]
    RecoveryOptions -->|2| ReviewData["Resume Modes, Review Data"]
    RecoveryOptions -->|3| CmdShutdown["Shutdown then Normal Startup"]
    InitHW --> DetermineLevel["Determine Achievable\nInteraction Levels 1-5"]
    ResumeAll --> DetermineLevel
    ReviewData --> DetermineLevel
    DetermineLevel --> OpsState{"Operations State"}
    OpsState --> NormalOps["Normal Operations Mode"]
    OpsState --> TrainingOps["Training Operations Mode"]
    OpsState --> MaintOps["Maintenance Operations Mode"]
    NormalOps --> MissionPlan["Mission Planning"]
    NormalOps --> MissionCtrl["Mission Control and Monitoring"]
    NormalOps --> PayloadMgmt["Payload Product Management"]
    NormalOps --> Targeting["Target Coord Development"]
    NormalOps --> C4IInterface["C4I Systems Interface"]
    MaintOps --> AVMaint["AV Maintenance"]
    MaintOps --> PayloadMaint["Payload Maintenance"]
    MaintOps --> DTMaint["Data Terminal Maintenance"]
    MaintOps --> FDL["Fault Detection/Location"]
    MaintOps --> SWUpgrade["Software Upgrade"]
    OpsState -->|Shutdown command| ShutdownState["Shutdown State\nStore/delete data, terminate interfaces"]
    ShutdownState --> PowerOff["Power Removed"]
flowchartDiagram.caption: TCS Operational States, Modes, and Functions Lifecycle

sequenceDiagram.syntaxExplanation: This sequence diagram shows a typical TCS mission execution workflow from planning through AV control to payload exploitation and C4I dissemination.
sequenceDiagram.code:
sequenceDiagram
    participant Op as UAV Operator
    participant TCS as TCS Workstation
    participant DT as Data Terminal
    participant AV as Air Vehicle
    participant C4I as C4I Systems

    Op->>TCS: Create Mission Plan
    TCS->>TCS: Display Map, Planning Tools
    Op->>TCS: Define 500 Waypoints, Payload Settings
    TCS->>TCS: Validate Mission Plan
    TCS-->>Op: Display discrepancies or success
    Op->>TCS: Upload Mission Plan to AV
    TCS->>DT: Transmit Flight/Payload Plan
    DT->>AV: Uplink Mission Data
    AV-->>DT: Acknowledge Receipt

    Note over Op,AV: Mission Execution Phase
    Op->>TCS: Command AV Launch
    TCS->>DT: Send Launch Commands
    DT->>AV: Uplink Launch Sequence
    AV-->>DT: Telemetry Downlink
    DT-->>TCS: AV Status, Position, Fuel
    TCS-->>Op: Display AV on Map, Status Panels

    Op->>TCS: Control Payload (EO/IR/SAR)
    TCS->>DT: Send Payload Commands
    DT->>AV: Uplink Payload Control
    AV-->>DT: Payload Data Downlink
    DT-->>TCS: EO/IR/SAR Imagery
    TCS->>TCS: Process NITF 2.0, Store, Display
    TCS-->>Op: Display Imagery with Overlays

    Op->>TCS: Mark Target, Request Coordinates
    TCS->>TCS: Compute Target Location + Accuracy
    Op->>TCS: Prepare Tactical Message
    TCS->>C4I: Disseminate NITF Imagery + Coordinates
    C4I-->>TCS: Acknowledge Receipt

    Note over TCS,AV: AV Deviates 10% from Path
    TCS-->>Op: WARNING - Flight Path Deviation
    Op->>TCS: Command Course Correction
    TCS->>DT: Send Navigation Update
    DT->>AV: Uplink Correction
sequenceDiagram.caption: TCS Mission Execution Workflow - Planning through Dissemination
