revisionHistory.description: Software Requirements Specification / Interface Requirements Specification for the X-38 Fault Tolerant System Services (FTSS) CSCI. Multiple revisions (A-F) due to updated FTPP specifications, NASA comments, and requirements traceability updates.
revisionHistory.author: The Charles Stark Draper Laboratory, Inc., Cambridge, Massachusetts. Contract No. NAS 9-97216. Prepared for: NASA Lyndon B. Johnson Space Center, Houston, Texas.

introduction.purpose: This SRS/IRS defines the software requirements and external interface requirements for the **Fault Tolerant System Services (FTSS)** Computer Software Configuration Item (CSCI) for NASA's X-38 Crew Return Vehicle. FTSS provides an Application Programming Interface (API) between NASA's application code and the underlying quad-redundant Flight Critical Computer (FCC) hardware based on Draper's Fault Tolerant Parallel Processor (FTPP) architecture. The document is directly traceable to the X-38 FTPP Requirements document (JSC 28671).
introduction.documentConventions: Requirements are identified by unique SRS identifiers (e.g., [SRS001]-[SRS304]) traceable to FTPP system requirements. Document follows MIL-STD-498, DI-IPSC-81433, and DI-IPSC-81434 guidance. Hardware requirements are marked 'hw' in traceability tables. Qualification methods include Demonstration (D), Test (T), Analysis (A), and Inspection (I).
introduction.intendedAudience: NASA Johnson Space Center X-38 program engineers, avionics system integrators, application software developers using the FTSS API, Draper Laboratory FTSS developers, safety/reliability engineers, and independent verification and validation teams.
introduction.productScope: **FTSS** is the core system software layer for the X-38 Crew Return Vehicle's Flight Critical Computer (FCC). It transforms raw hardware (Motorola PowerPC 604R processors, Draper Network Elements, I/O devices) into a real-time, fault-tolerant computing system. FTSS provides: Scheduling Services (50/10/1 Hz rate groups), Communication Services (synchronous message queues and immediate pipes with Byzantine resilience), Time Services (MET and SEP), Memory Management (scrubbing, protection, alignment), Fault Detection and Isolation (IBIT and CBIT), Redundancy Management (degradation, recovery, reintegration), System Support Services (telemetry, command), and Power Down Services. FTSS runs on both FCPs (full set) and ICPs (reduced communication services subset).
references: MIL-STD-498 Software Development and Documentation, 5 December 1994
DI-IPSC-81433 Software Requirements Specification DID, 5 December 1996
DI-IPSC-81434 Interface Requirements Specification DID, 5 December 1996
JSC 28671 X-38 Fault Tolerant Parallel Processor Requirements, Rev 6.2, 13 April 2001
Draper Doc 297752 Application Programming Interface for X-38 FTSS
Draper Doc 297746 Certification Test Procedure for Network Element
MPCC01 Firmware Manual Rev A, Radstone Technology PLC
MPCC01 Hardware Manual Rev B, Radstone Technology PLC
VxWorks Reference Manual, 5.4 Edition 1, Wind River Systems

overallDescription.productPerspective: FTSS is a critical middleware layer within the X-38 FCC avionics architecture. The FCC consists of four Flight Critical Processors (FCPs) operating as a quad-redundant Virtual Group, five simplex Instrument Control Processors (ICPs) as separate VGs, five Draper Network Elements (NEs) providing Byzantine-resilient communication, four Multi-protocol/RS-422 cards (MPCCs), sixteen Digital I/O cards, four Analog I/O cards, and four Decomm cards. FTSS sits between VxWorks 5.4 RTOS and NASA's flight application software, abstracting the fault tolerance hardware complexity into a clean API. The NEs implement Byzantine Resilient Virtual Circuit (BRVC) communications using fiber-optic interconnects between fault containment regions.
productFunctions: System Initialization: Transform hardware into synchronized, fault-tolerant real-time system with watchdog timer, FCP virtual group configuration, ICP simplex VG setup, and application initialization
Scheduling Services: Rate-group based deterministic task execution at 50 Hz, 10 Hz, and 1 Hz with vehicle mode management, overrun detection, and helper tasks
Communication Services: Synchronous message queues and immediate pipe sockets with Byzantine-resilient data exchange, broadcast capability, and babbling node detection/masking
Memory Management: RAM scrubbing with EDAC, memory protection categorization (congruent aligned/initialized, non-congruent), and memory alignment during channel recovery
Fault Detection and Isolation: Initial BIT (FCP, ICP, PMC1553, MPCC hardware tests) and Continuous BIT (50 Hz presence test, watchdog reset, fault diagnosis within 1 second)
Redundancy Management: Virtual group configuration, degradation strategies (quad→triplex→degraded triplex), processor reintegration, NE recovery, voted resets, and power cycle management
Time Services: Mission Elapsed Time (MET) and Separation Elapsed Time (SEP) with 50 PPM accuracy, rate-group-specific resolution, and utility timer
System Support Services: Telemetry transmission (12,800 bytes/10Hz frame) and command reception via CTC/MPCC with redundant path switching

userClassesAndCharacteristics.characteristics: Uses the FTSS API (Draper Doc 297752) to build X-38 flight control software. Creates tasks, rate groups, vehicle modes, and communication sockets. Must understand congruent vs non-congruent memory semantics. Responsible for vehicle/mission management including enabling/disabling processor recovery and alignment.
Integrates FTSS with VxWorks, application code, and FCC hardware. Manages build/download/flash programming processes using Tornado 2 for NT. Validates telemetry budget compliance and CPU utilization.
Verifies FTSS meets two-fault-tolerance requirements across all X-38 mission phases. Analyzes fault containment, recovery timing, and Byzantine resilience guarantees.

overallDescription.operatingEnvironment: Embedded real-time environment on Radstone PowerPC 604R processors within the X-38 FCC. VxWorks 5.4 RTOS. Quad-redundant FCP configuration with five NEs providing Byzantine-resilient fiber-optic communication. Space environment with radiation hardened/tolerant components. Operating temperature 0°C to 65°C at card edge, storage -30°C to 60°C. Conduction-cooled 6U VME boards in ATR chassis. Must withstand random vibration (14.1 g-rms qualification), ionizing radiation per SSP 30512 Rev. C, 20g sawtooth shock, and 90% humidity non-condensing.
designAndImplementationConstraints: Written in C programming language [SRS159]
VxWorks 5.4 operating system [SRS160]
FTSS + VxWorks ≤ 3 MB ROM [SRS193]
FTSS + VxWorks ≤ 9 MB DRAM; only 4 MB realigned during recovery [SRS258, SRS259]
FCP load modules must be identical across all four FCPs [SRS166]
Compiled/linked/downloaded using Tornado 2 for NT [SRS253]
Delivered via CD-ROM [SRS252]
Asymmetric I/O calls must not cause desynchronization [SRS168]
VMEbus single block transfer ≤ 100 microseconds [SRS223]
Telemetry budget: ≤ 5000 bits/second + 600 bits startup data [SRS250, SRS280]
userDocumentation: FTSS SRS/IRS (this document)
Application Programming Interface document (Draper 297752)
Certification Test Procedures for Network Element (Draper 297746)
Software Users Manual with release notes
assumptionsAndDependencies: FCC hardware (Radstone PowerPC 604R, NEs, MPCCs, DIO, Analog I/O, Decomm cards) is available and functional
VxWorks 5.4 Board Support Package (BSP) is provided by manufacturer
Network Elements automatically enter ISYNC on power-up
Minimum 3 of 5 FCRs must be active for FCP virtual group formation
Application software manages vehicle modes and alignment permission policy
Two Command and Telemetry Computers (CTCs) are available for telemetry/command paths

externalInterfaceRequirements.userInterfaces: No direct user interface. FTSS provides an API for application software interaction as documented in Draper Document 297752.
externalInterfaceRequirements.hardwareInterfaces: Radstone PowerPC 604R processors (FCP and ICP). Draper Network Elements providing Byzantine-resilient fiber-optic communication. Multi-Protocol Communications Controllers (MPCCs) for RS-422 serial telemetry/command at 2,097,152 bps (telemetry) and 1,048,576 bps (command) in SDLC mode. VME backplane bus for FCP-ICP shared memory and interrupt communication. ISA Bridge hardware timer for 50 Hz scheduling. Command and Telemetry Computers (CTCs) via MPCC RS-422 links.
externalInterfaceRequirements.softwareInterfaces: VxWorks 5.4 RTOS (see VxWorks Reference Manual for interface details; Appendix A of API defines allowable VxWorks call subset). FTSS provides API services for: task creation, rate group management, vehicle mode management, socket creation/open/send/receive, time queries, health status queries, exception handling, recovery control, and telemetry/command operations.
externalInterfaceRequirements.communicationsInterfaces: Network Element BRVC: 64-byte packet exchanges with descriptor blocks (packet class, toVID, fromVID, user field) and syndrome reports (vote errors, clock errors, link errors, OBNE/IBNF timeouts, scoreboard vote errors, timestamps). FCP-ICP: VMEbus shared memory + IRQ5 interrupt for 50 Hz synchronization (≤330 µs skew). MPCC: RS-422 serial links to CTCs in SDLC mode with CRC. Data rates: NE Class 1/2 ≥ 1 MB/s bandwidth.

systemFeatures.name: System Initialization
systemFeatures.description: Transforms hardware into a synchronized fault-tolerant real-time system. Configures Boot ROM, performs IBIT, initializes VxWorks and FTSS, synchronizes FCPs in presence of 2.5s power-on skew, configures virtual groups, and starts the 50 Hz timer. Must complete within 1.5 minutes. Priority: Essential (flight safety critical).
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: Power-on reset. Response: Boot ROM executes IBIT, calls VxWorks BSP init, then FTSS System Init. FTSS enables watchdog, synchronizes FCPs (≥3 of 5 active), aligns processor state, configures ICP VGs, calls application init, sends FCP Ready Sync, waits 2.5s for ICP Ready, starts 50 Hz timer.
Stimulus: FCP fails to sync within 2.5s. Response: Surviving triplex sends voted VMEbus reset to failed FCP via NE within 1 second.
Stimulus: NEFU ICP fails to send Ready signal. Response: FTSS masks out that ICP but continues using the NE.
functionalRequirements: SRS194: On power-on reset, perform all initialization functions
SRS234: Boot ROM configured to complete IBIT, then call VxWorks BSP followed by FTSS System Init
SRS014, SRS292: Initiate and reset watchdog timer so it does not expire in absence of fault
SRS008: Synchronize FCP virtual group with 2.5-second power-on skew tolerance
SRS010: Configure FCP VG using all available synchronized processors (minimum 3 of 5 FCRs)
SRS177: Surviving triplex attempts sync with failed FCP
SRS178: Send voted VMEbus reset if FCP not synced within 2.5 seconds
SRS011: Align processor state and congruent aligned memory (registers, timers)
SRS296: Configure ICP simplex virtual groups for each channel
SRS297: Wait up to 15 seconds for ICP communication after VG configuration
SRS215: Call application initialization function for tasks, sockets, vehicle mode, memory alignment
SRS221: Send FCP Ready Sync to ICP after application init complete
SRS189: Wait up to 2.5 seconds for ICP Ready signal
SRS243: Mask out NEFU ICP if it fails to send Ready signal, but continue using NE
SRS199: Start 50 Hz timer and enable timer interrupt when all activities complete
SRS015: Complete initialization within 1.5 minutes from hardware reset

systemFeatures.name: Scheduling Services
systemFeatures.description: Deterministic rate-group-based task scheduling at 50 Hz, 10 Hz, and 1 Hz. Supports up to 5 vehicle modes with up to 3 rate groups each and up to 20 tasks per rate group. Includes overrun detection, mode change management, helper tasks, and task deadline capability. Priority: Essential.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: 50 Hz timer interrupt occurs. Response: Scheduler invokes, updates minor frame count (0-49), unblocks enabled tasks in priority order per rate group.
Stimulus: Application requests vehicle mode change. Response: Scheduler completes change within 1.02 seconds, processing during minor frame 49.
Stimulus: Rate group overrun detected. Response: Scheduler reports via API, provides mechanism to identify overrunning task, restarts task at beginning.
functionalRequirements: SRS017: API call to install task into rate group at system init
SRS196: Support up to 20 tasks per rate group
SRS018: API call to install rate group into vehicle mode at system init
SRS197: Support up to 3 rate groups per vehicle mode
SRS195: Support up to 5 vehicle modes
SRS002: Provide identical services in all vehicle modes
SRS019-021: API for mode change notification; complete within 1.02 seconds; process during minor frame 49
SRS022: Execute cyclic tasks with API call for task blocking
SRS024: Execute as highest priority FTSS/application task
SRS025: Maintain minor frame count 0 to 49
SRS027: Task priority based on rate (higher rate = higher priority)
SRS028-029: Detect 50/10/1 Hz overruns; report via API for telemetry
SRS035: Rate groups execute at 50/10/1 Hz with ≤50 µs/s drift and ≤330 µs jitter
SRS034: ≤1 ms FTSS overhead per 50 Hz frame (assuming 27 packets)
SRS181: Set 50 Hz timer congruently across all operational FCPs
SRS032: Issue 50 Hz VMEbus IRQ5 interrupt to ICPs
SRS191: ICP interrupt skew ≤330 microseconds
SRS033: Send minor frame number, vehicle mode, MET, SEP to ICP before 50 Hz interrupt
SRS042: Helper tasks for sub-rate 10/1 Hz source congruency and voted output exchanges
SRS270: Task deadline capability specifying start and finish minor frames

systemFeatures.name: Communication Services
systemFeatures.description: Message-passing capabilities layered on Byzantine Resilient Virtual Circuit (BRVC) network. Provides synchronous message queues (frame-boundary exchanges) and immediate pipes (low-latency transfers). Supports broadcast, intra-VG bypass, babbling node detection/masking, and guaranteed message ordering. Priority: Essential.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: Task sends synchronous message via message queue. Response: Message queued for transmission at next rate group frame boundary; delivered congruently to all VG members.
Stimulus: 50 Hz FCP task sends immediate pipe message to ICP. Response: Message transferred immediately via NE; ICP can poll until read.
Stimulus: Babbling NE/ICP detected. Response: FTSS detects within 20 ms, masks out within 40 ms after detection.
functionalRequirements: SRS047: Provide synchronous message passing via message queues
SRS048: Provide immediate message passing via pipes
SRS049: Provide broadcast capability to all virtual groups
SRS050: Restrict immediate inter-VG messaging to highest rate group on FCP (not ICPs)
SRS051: Detect intra-VG messages and bypass network element
SRS052: Route messages to proper VG(s) and socket
SRS053: Deliver messages in same order at each VG member
SRS054: Perform synchronous passing at rate group frame boundaries
SRS055: Synchronous sockets queue outgoing messages until frame boundaries
SRS059: Return error if insufficient space to enqueue message (non-blocking)
SRS062-066: Message queue API with guaranteed delivery, error handling, single sender/receiver per VG
SRS067-073: Pipe API with immediate messaging, broadcast pipes, error handling
SRS235: Detect babbling NE/ICP within 20 ms of first erroneous packet
SRS255: Mask out babbling NE/ICP within 40 ms after detection

systemFeatures.name: Fault Detection and Isolation
systemFeatures.description: IBIT (Initial Built-In Test) at startup for FCPs, ICPs, PMC1553s, and MPCCs. CBIT (Continuous BIT) at 50 Hz during operations for presence testing, watchdog management, and fault diagnosis. RAM scrubbing for EDAC-triggered error correction. Priority: Essential (safety critical).
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: System power-on. Response: FTSS configures Radstone firmware to execute MPE, Power-up, and IBIT tests per component tables. MPE failure halts processing; other failures allow continued processing with error logging.
Stimulus: CBIT presence test detects FCP desynchronization. Response: Diagnose faulty FCR within 1 second, report to application via telemetry.
Stimulus: ICP heartbeat missing for 2 consecutive minor cycles. Response: CBIT declares ICP failed and reports to application.
functionalRequirements: SRS237: Configure FCP as Radstone IBIT master (except NEFU ICP is master)
SRS260-262: FCP IBIT — MPE tests halt on failure; Power-up/IBIT tests continue on failure
SRS287-289: ICP IBIT — same policy as FCP
SRS264-266: ICP/PMC1553 IBIT — MPE halt, IBIT continue on failure
SRS267: MPCC MPE tests halt on failure
SRS239: Report IBIT results for all boards to application via telemetry
SRS269: IBIT halt failures handled via Recovery procedures
SRS290: Non-halting IBIT failures trigger FCR recovery actions
SRS093: CBIT executes on FCP virtual group
SRS094: Reset watchdog timer at 50 Hz (1.6s nominal timeout)
SRS095: 50 Hz presence test ensures FCP synchronization
SRS184: Presence test verifies all processors execute same frames
SRS096: Diagnose faulty FCR within 1 second of detection
SRS097: Detect failed ICP by 2 consecutive missed heartbeat messages
SRS091: CBIT + RM + Scheduler ≤2 ms under nominal no-fault conditions
SRS183: CBIT + RM + Scheduler ≤3 ms while processing faults
SRS043-044: RAM scrub cyclically reads/writes all used RAM; reports errors congruently
SRS187: Scrub ≥10 MB in 8 minutes using ≤1% CPU
SRS275: RAM scrub excludes telemetry data area

systemFeatures.name: Redundancy Management
systemFeatures.description: Maintains physical-to-virtual group mapping. Manages degradation from quad to triplex to degraded triplex. Handles processor and NE recovery including voted resets, power cycle requests, memory alignment, and reintegration. Application controls alignment permission. Priority: Essential (safety critical).
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: FCR failure diagnosed (quad config). Response: Degrade to triplex within 3 minor frames; remove FCR from NE Configuration Table; initiate recovery if alignment permitted.
Stimulus: Recovery attempt — voted reset sent. Response: Wait 1.5 minutes; if not recovered, request application power cycle; if second cycle fails, declare permanently failed.
Stimulus: Alignment permitted after recovery. Response: Synchronize channel at 1 Hz rate; align memory (≤1 s/MB congruent data) on major frame boundary; reintegrate within 1.5 minutes.
functionalRequirements: SRS099: API to retrieve health status of processors, NEs, links, MPCCs, ICP interfaces
SRS100: API to request voted reset of a channel
SRS101: Define initial mapping: 1 quadruplex FCP VG + 5 ICP simplexes
SRS201: Accommodate power-up of all 5 channels maintaining all 5 NEs active
SRS106: Quad→triplex degradation on FCR failure (non-NEFU), removing NE and processors
SRS282: Triplex→degraded triplex with 4 NEs active on second FCR failure
SRS284: Mask out processors if only 3 NEs active on additional failure
SRS254: No action on FCR failure in degraded triplex
SRS245: NEFU first failure — mask processor, keep NE in config
SRS283: NEFU subsequent errors with ≥4 NEs — remove NE, attempt recovery
SRS109: Degrade FCP VG within 3 minor frames of fault detection
SRS204: Issue voted reset if alignment permitted
SRS129: Initiate transient NE recovery for Byzantine-resilient communications
SRS110: Reintegrate failed FCP when alignment permitted and failure not permanent
SRS205: Recovery complete within 1.5 minutes from failure identification
SRS208-209: Request power cycle at 1.5-minute intervals if voted reset fails
SRS211: Declare permanently failed after power cycle requests fail
SRS285: API to reset permanently failed channel to initial recovery state
SRS302: API to specify whether recovery/alignment permitted
SRS123-126: Maintain synchronous operations during reintegration; periodic 1-second re-sync attempts; memory alignment on major frame boundary
SRS214: Incorporate new channel within 1.5 minutes of power application
SRS236: Serially incorporate two simultaneously powered channels
SRS046, SRS217: Define memory categorization and map for congruent aligned/initialized/non-congruent
SRS045, SRS186, SRS200, SRS203: Memory alignment — align state, write voted values, initialize from NVM, ≤1 s/MB

systemFeatures.name: Time Services
systemFeatures.description: Mission Elapsed Time (MET) and Separation Elapsed Time (SEP) with rate-group-specific resolution, congruent across all FCPs. Utility timer for application use. Priority: Essential.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: First 50 Hz frame after initialization. Response: MET initialized to zero, begins incrementing.
Stimulus: Application notifies FTSS of vehicle release from Shuttle RMS. Response: SEP initialized to zero, begins incrementing next frame.
Stimulus: Application requests utility timer value. Response: Returns non-voted timer with ≤60.6 ns resolution, reset at each major frame start.
functionalRequirements: SRS142: Provide MET and SEP with resolution: 20 ms (50 Hz), 100 ms (10 Hz), 1 s (1 Hz)
SRS218, SRS219: MET and SEP drift rate ≤50 PPM
SRS144: MET does not rollover for 30 days
SRS145: SEP does not rollover for 1 day
SRS165: MET initialized to zero at first 50 Hz frame
SRS161: SEP initialized to zero on RMS release notification, starts next frame
SRS246-248: Utility timer assigned to non-congruent memory, accuracy ≤50 PPM, reset to zero before first task each major frame
SRS256: Utility timer resolution ≤60.6 nanoseconds

systemFeatures.name: System Support Services
systemFeatures.description: Telemetry logging and command reception via MPCC/CTC interfaces with redundant path switching. Transfers 12,800 bytes per 10 Hz frame. Priority: Essential.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: 10 Hz frame boundary. Response: FTSS transfers telemetry buffer to MPCC/CTC within 5.2 ms; signals MPCC to send to CTC. Checks for command messages from both CTCs.
Stimulus: MPCC transmission error 10 consecutive times. Response: Switch to redundant MPCC device; continue close/reopen of faulty MPCC until recovered.
functionalRequirements: SRS148: Transfer 12,800 bytes within 10 Hz frame from FCP
SRS149-150: Transfer telemetry block to FCC-MPCC and signal CTC transfer
SRS300: Provide MPCC link status data to FDI
SRS151: API to specify telemetry buffer address and length
SRS257: ≤5.2 ms FCP processing time for telemetry operations
SRS152-153: Check for and read command data from each CTC at 10 Hz
SRS304: Provide command link status to application
SRS156: API to provide current command data
SRS298-299: After 10 consecutive errors, switch to redundant MPCC
SRS242: Continue close/reopen of faulty MPCC until recovery
SRS222: Choose error-free FCC-MPCC path, switching channels as necessary
SRS286: API to specify which MPCC channels for telemetry/command

performanceRequirements: SRS015: System Initialization ≤1.5 minutes from hardware reset to 50 Hz timer start
SRS034: ≤1 ms FTSS overhead per 50 Hz frame (with 27 packets)
SRS091: CBIT + RM + Scheduler ≤2 ms per minor frame (no-fault)
SRS183: CBIT + RM + Scheduler ≤3 ms per minor frame (processing faults)
SRS035: Rate group execution with ≤50 µs/s drift and ≤330 µs jitter
SRS109: Degrade FCP VG within 3 minor frames (60 ms) of fault detection
SRS096: Diagnose faulty FCR within 1 second
SRS205: Channel recovery ≤1.5 minutes
SRS203: Memory alignment ≤1 second per megabyte
SRS235: Detect babbling NE/ICP within 20 ms
SRS255: Mask babbling NE/ICP within 40 ms of detection
SRS223: VMEbus single block transfer ≤100 µs
SRS257: Telemetry processing ≤5.2 ms per 10 Hz frame
safetyRequirements: Two-fault tolerance for any two non-simultaneous hardware faults across all X-38 mission phases
No single hardware failure shall result in loss of vehicle control
Watchdog timer active during all phases including memory re-alignment
Byzantine-resilient communication via Network Elements prevents arbitrary fault propagation
securityRequirements: No specific security requirements identified (FTSS is embedded flight software)
softwareQualityAttributes: Deterministic behavior for all synchronous tasks
Identical load modules across all four FCPs [SRS166]
Congruent message delivery across all VG members [SRS053]
Congruent error recording even for locally-detected RAM scrub errors [SRS044]
Non-blocking socket operations [SRS059]
Recovery does not depend on specific channel hardware assignment
businessRules: FTSS provides identical services in all vehicle modes [SRS002]
Application controls alignment permission — FTSS defers to Vehicle/Mission Manager policy
NEFU ICP presence/absence does not affect FTSS software load
Asymmetric I/O calls must not induce desynchronization [SRS168]

otherRequirements: SRS158: Execute on Radstone Power PC 604R
SRS159: Written in C programming language
SRS160: Use VxWorks Operating System version 5.4
SRS193: FTSS + VxWorks ≤3 MB ROM
SRS258: FTSS + VxWorks ≤9 MB DRAM
SRS259: Only 4 MB DRAM realigned during recovery
SRS253: Compiled with Tornado 2 for NT
SRS252: Delivered via CD-ROM
SRS249: Power down API to close all rate groups, delete all sockets and tasks
ICP Services: API calls for channel identification, status messaging, pipe communication, minor frame/MET/SEP retrieval, alignment notification

glossary.definition: Fault Tolerant System Services — core middleware providing scheduling, communication, fault tolerance, and redundancy management for the X-38 FCC
Fault Tolerant Parallel Processor — Draper's quad-redundant processor architecture used in the X-38 FCC
Flight Critical Computer — the quad-redundant avionics computer for the X-38 Crew Return Vehicle
Flight Critical Processor — one of four Radstone PowerPC 604R processors forming the FCC quad-redundant virtual group
Instrument Control Processor — one of five simplex processors managing I/O devices in the FCC
Network Element — Draper-designed hardware providing Byzantine-resilient fiber-optic communication between fault containment regions
Fault Containment Region — an electrically isolated, independently powered and clocked hardware partition containing an NE and 1-2 processors
Virtual Group — a logical grouping of physical processors into a computational site; can be simplex or redundant (fault masking)
Byzantine Resilient Virtual Circuit — abstracted view of NEs and fiber network providing guaranteed message ordering and consistency under arbitrary faults
Network Element Fifth Unit — the fifth FCR containing an NE and ICP but no FCP; provides additional NE for Byzantine resilience
Initial Built-In Test — hardware self-tests executed at power-on/reset for FCPs, ICPs, PMC1553s, and MPCCs
Continuous Built-In Test — ongoing 50 Hz presence tests and watchdog management during operations
Minimum Processing Environment — critical hardware tests that halt processing on failure (PROM, Flash, RAM, Universe)
Mission Elapsed Time — congruent timer initialized to zero at first 50 Hz frame, ≤50 PPM drift, 30-day rollover
Separation Elapsed Time — congruent timer for post-separation timing, initialized on RMS release notification, 1-day rollover
Transient Network Element Recovery — process to bring a failed NE back into synchronization with the working group
Initial Synchronization — NE microcode process to achieve clock synchronization at power-up
Multi-Protocol Communications Controller — Radstone RS-422 card for telemetry and command serial communication
Command and Telemetry Computer — external computer connected to MPCC for telemetry capture and command uplink
Fault consisting of arbitrary behavior by failed components, including sending conflicting information to different destinations
A processor or NE that continually unexpectedly sends messages over the fiber network, overloading it with unnecessary traffic
Configuration with FCR having 2 processors but ≥3 NEs, with a third processor masked out to maintain the VG
Process of bringing a desynchronized processor back into synchronization with other members of its redundant virtual group
Reset applied to an NE as the result of other FCP processors agreeing to use the NE voted reset function to reset a faulty channel
Process of synchronizing congruent memory, registers, and timers of a recovered channel with the operational channels
Part of the NE hardware that acts as the message scheduler, collectively deciding with redundant NEs when messages are ready

flowchartDiagram.syntaxExplanation: This flowchart shows the FTSS states (Initialization, Normal Operation, Fault Recovery) and the primary transitions between them.
flowchartDiagram.code:
flowchart TD
    PowerOn["Power Applied / Reset"] --> SysInit["System Initialization"]
    SysInit --> BootROM["Boot ROM: IBIT → VxWorks BSP → FTSS Init"]
    BootROM --> WatchDog["Enable Watchdog Timer"]
    WatchDog --> Sync["Synchronize FCP VG\n2.5s power-on skew tolerance\nMinimum 3 of 5 FCRs"]
    Sync --> AlignState["Align Processor State\nRegisters, Timers, Memory"]
    AlignState --> ConfigICP["Configure 5 ICP Simplex VGs\nWait 15s for ICP Init"]
    ConfigICP --> AppInit["Application Initialization\nCreate Tasks, Sockets, Modes"]
    AppInit --> FCPReady["Send FCP Ready Sync → ICP\nWait 2.5s for ICP Ready"]
    FCPReady --> Start50Hz["Start 50 Hz Timer\nEnable Timer Interrupt"]
    Start50Hz --> NormalOp["Normal Operation"]
    NormalOp -->|Fault Detected| FaultRecovery["Fault Recovery"]
    NormalOp -->|Reset Exception| SysInit
    FaultRecovery --> Degrade["Degrade VG within\n3 minor frames"]
    Degrade --> VotedReset["Voted Reset to\nFailed Channel"]
    VotedReset --> WaitRecover{"Recovered within\n1.5 minutes?"}
    WaitRecover -->|Yes| Reintegrate["Reintegrate Processor\nMemory Alignment"]
    WaitRecover -->|No| PowerCycle["Request App\nPower Cycle"]
    PowerCycle --> WaitRecover2{"Recovered after\n2nd cycle?"}
    WaitRecover2 -->|Yes| Reintegrate
    WaitRecover2 -->|No| PermFail["Declare Permanently\nFailed Channel"]
    Reintegrate --> NormalOp
    PermFail --> NormalOp
flowchartDiagram.caption: FTSS Operational States and Fault Recovery Lifecycle

sequenceDiagram.syntaxExplanation: This sequence diagram shows the system initialization flow and the 50 Hz scheduling cycle including FCP-ICP communication.
sequenceDiagram.code:
sequenceDiagram
    participant ROM as Boot ROM
    participant FCP as FCP Processors
    participant FTSS as FTSS Software
    participant NE as Network Elements
    participant ICP as Instrument Control Processors
    participant App as Application Software

    Note over ROM,ICP: System Initialization Phase
    ROM->>ROM: Execute IBIT (MPE, Power-up, Initial BIT)
    ROM->>FCP: Call VxWorks BSP Init
    FCP->>FTSS: Call FTSS System Init
    FTSS->>FTSS: Enable and reset watchdog timer
    FTSS->>NE: ISYNC — synchronize NEs via fiber
    NE-->>FTSS: NE synchronization complete
    FTSS->>FTSS: Synchronize FCP VG (≥3 of 5 FCRs)
    FTSS->>FTSS: Align processor state and memory
    FTSS->>NE: Configure ICP simplex VGs
    FTSS->>App: Call application init function
    App->>FTSS: Create tasks, sockets, vehicle modes
    FTSS->>ICP: Send FCP Ready Sync
    ICP-->>FTSS: ICP Ready signal (within 2.5s)
    FTSS->>FTSS: Start 50 Hz timer, enable interrupt

    Note over FCP,ICP: Normal Operation — 50 Hz Cycle
    loop Every 20ms Minor Frame
        FTSS->>FTSS: 50 Hz timer interrupt
        FTSS->>ICP: Write minor frame, mode, MET, SEP to shared memory
        FTSS->>ICP: Issue VMEbus IRQ5 (≤330µs skew)
        FTSS->>App: Execute 50 Hz rate group tasks
        App->>FTSS: Send/receive messages via sockets
        FTSS->>NE: Exchange packets (BRVC)
        NE-->>FTSS: Voted data + syndromes
        FTSS->>FTSS: CBIT presence test + watchdog reset
        FTSS->>FTSS: Redundancy Management overhead
    end
sequenceDiagram.caption: FTSS Initialization and 50 Hz Scheduling Cycle

entityRelationshipDiagram.syntaxExplanation: This ER diagram shows the key architectural entities in the FTSS system and their relationships.
entityRelationshipDiagram.caption: FTSS Architecture Entity Relationships

dataDictionary.definition: 8-bit NE descriptor field selecting the data exchange primitive (Class 0, 1, 2, or Broadcast)
NE syndrome indicating disagreement during data exchange among FCP VG members
NE syndrome indicating fault-tolerant clock synchronization outside allowable skew window
NE syndrome indicating fiber-optic link error between NEs
Output Buffer Not Empty — NE syndrome when an FCP member fails to transmit within the time skew window
Input Buffer Not Full — NE syndrome when an FCP member fails to free input buffer space within the time skew window
Memory that is always aligned (synchronized) across all FCP VG members during recovery
Memory initialized from non-volatile storage during channel recovery
Memory that is never aligned during recovery, used for channel-unique information

revisionHistory.description: Deliverable D8: Functional Specification and Architecture for the BEYOND project — ITEA 99002 EUREKA 2023. Summarizes progress from first to second milestone, detailing functional specifications for adaptive, multimodal user interfaces across four application domains.
revisionHistory.author: Edited by K. Coninx (LUC-EDM). Contributors: F. Schinagl (APC Interactive Solutions AG), B. Eggen and B. De Ruyter (Philips Research USIT), L. Loomans (Philips Hasselt), J. Devos (BARCO BarcoView), M. Mulder (TU Delft). Published via BEYOND Website.

introduction.purpose: This document (Deliverable D8) summarizes the progress in the BEYOND project and clarifies the approach for going from the first to the second milestone prototype. It serves as a second iteration on D2 'Requirements and Usability Methodology', providing functional specifications for the second prototype to be delivered at milestone two. The BEYOND project investigates **user-centred intelligence** through adaptive, multimodal graphical user interfaces across multiple application domains.
introduction.documentConventions: Domain-oriented structure with each project covering: introduction, project status, usability methods/results, functional specifications (general requirements, usability, adaptivity, multimodality, simulation), and architectural issues. Key aspects (multimodality, adaptivity, simulation, usability) treated as independent variables across all domains. Related to deliverables D1 (key concepts), D2 (requirements), D3 (Common Adaptivity Reference Framework).
introduction.intendedAudience: BEYOND consortium partners, ITEA programme reviewers, researchers in adaptive user interfaces, multimodal interaction, and usability engineering; industrial partners in public terminal, consumer electronics, vetronics, and avionics domains.
introduction.productScope:
**BEYOND** (ITEA 99002) is a multi-domain research project investigating adaptive, multimodal user interfaces with four key workpackages: WP1 (Multimodality), WP2 (Adaptivity), WP3 (Simulation), and WP4 (Usability). The project spans four application domains:

1. **Public Domain**: Accesspoint information terminals/kiosks with speech and visual interaction for public spaces
2. **Home Domain**: (a) Future in-home electronic systems (Home Experience, Multimodal Jukebox); (b) UI Editor/authoring tool for consumer product on-screen interfaces (e.g., DVD players)
3. **Vetronics Domain**: UI Editor for ruggedized vehicle-electronics displays with plug-in architecture and virtual machine firmware
4. **Avionics Domain**: Intelligent Adaptive Flight Deck with multi-agent architecture integrating GPWS and TCAS warning systems

Each domain develops first and second prototypes, progressing from basic to extended functionality with lessons learned from usability evaluation.
references: D1 — BEYOND Key Concepts
D2 — Requirements and Usability Methodology
D3 — Common Adaptivity Reference Framework
Abeloos, A.L.M. (2000) The Intelligent Adaptive Flight Deck, TU Delft + BARCO
Dieterich, H. et al. (1993) State of the Art in Adaptive User Interfaces
Reason, J. (1987) Generic Error-Modelling System (GEMS)
Steentjes, A. and Mulder, M. (2000) Analysis of a Questionnaire addressing Free Flight's Big Picture Displays

overallDescription.productPerspective: BEYOND is a European ITEA/EUREKA consortium research project (1999-2001) developing next-generation user interfaces that are adaptive, multimodal, and validated through simulation and usability testing. It builds upon industrial applications ranging from public information kiosks to safety-critical flight deck systems. The project follows a two-milestone approach: first prototypes demonstrate basic functionality, second prototypes incorporate lessons learned, extended adaptivity, and improved architecture. The Common Adaptivity Reference Framework (D3) provides a shared conceptual model used across domains.
productFunctions: Multimodal interaction combining speech, visual, touch, and graphical interfaces for diverse application domains
Adaptive user interfaces that adjust to user context, preferences, and environmental conditions (online and offline adaptivity)
Simulation of user interface prototypes for early validation and usability testing
Usability engineering through expert reviews, questionnaires, log file analysis, and video surveillance
UI authoring tools for rapid development of on-screen interfaces for consumer and vehicle-electronics products
Intelligent agent-based systems for safety-critical decision support in avionics

userClassesAndCharacteristics.characteristics: General public in everyday circumstances. No prior training required. May include all ages, genders, and abilities. Interact with accesspoint kiosks using speech, touch, and visual channels. Speaker-independent voice recognition required. Must be attracted and guided by the system proactively.

End users interacting with in-home entertainment systems (audio-video, DVD players). Expect intuitive, customizable interfaces. Use remote controls and on-screen displays. Limited technical expertise assumed.

Professional developers using the authoring tool to create, simulate, and deploy on-screen user interfaces for consumer products. Familiar with Windows-based development environments. Need wizard assistance and WYSIWYG design capabilities.

Engineers creating user interfaces for ruggedized displays in vehicles (ships, trains, trams, construction equipment). Must support event-driven programming, plug-in hardware modules, and system configuration. Use the UI Editor to design, compile, download, and simulate UIs before deployment to embedded systems.

Professional aviators operating in complex, safety-critical environments. 65% of aircraft incidents are caused by human error. Require adaptive displays that present the right information in the right format at the right time. Must maintain situation awareness, detect/correct errors, and make optimized decisions under high workload.

userClassesAndCharacteristics.userClass: Avionics Engineers and Flight Test Engineers
userClassesAndCharacteristics.characteristics: Domain experts evaluating adaptive flight deck prototypes through questionnaires and expert reviews. Assess pilot acceptability, workload, situation awareness, and display effectiveness.

overallDescription.operatingEnvironment: Multi-platform deployment: Public terminals run on MS Windows with multimedia capabilities. Consumer UI Editor runs on standard PCs (Windows 98/ME/2000). Vetronics firmware runs on ruggedized embedded systems with virtual machine for UI interpretation, communicating via Serial, USB, FireWire, IrDA, CAN bus. Avionics prototype runs on Windows NT workstation using Java (JACK Intelligent Agents) and OpenGL for display rendering. Target environments range from well-controlled indoor spaces to extreme conditions (vibration, temperature, noise, humidity) in vehicle and aircraft cockpits.
designAndImplementationConstraints: Consumer domain authoring tool must account for limited RAM, no hard disk, and slow processors on target platforms
Vetronics UI Editor must use plug-in component-based architecture (COM/COM+) for hardware extensibility
Vetronics firmware requires virtual machine closely matched with hardware for performance
Avionics system uses multi-agent architecture with JACK Intelligent Agents and OpenGL
Public terminal system requires MS Windows platform for multimedia capabilities
All domains must support both first and second prototype development within 2-year project timeline
userDocumentation: BEYOND Deliverable D1 — Key Concepts
BEYOND Deliverable D2 — Requirements and Usability Methodology
BEYOND Deliverable D3 — Common Adaptivity Reference Framework
BEYOND Deliverable D8 — Functional Specification and Architecture (this document)
Individual workpackage deliverables for multimodality, adaptivity, and simulation details
assumptionsAndDependencies: German funding situation uncertainty caused loss of experienced usability workpackage partners; other partners compensated by devoting own resources to usability
First prototypes serve as validated baselines for second prototype functional specifications
Common Adaptivity Reference Framework (D3) is available for integration into domain architectures
Hardware platforms for each domain are available and reaching definitive form
Two-year research consortium timeline constrains depth of longitudinal usability studies

externalInterfaceRequirements.userInterfaces:
**Public Domain**: Multimodal accesspoint terminal with graphical user interface (2D abstract with virtual characters), speech input/output, visual surveillance for user detection, and context-aware dialog management. Window-based display with information flow compatible with average user cognitive capabilities.

**Home Domain**: On-screen display (OSD) for consumer products with standard widgets (screens, dialogs, text, buttons, sliders, pictures), color palettes, bitmap images, and menu navigation. Remote control interaction.

**Vetronics Domain**: Ruggedized display with OSD overlay, symbol generator, touchscreen hotspots, event-action associations, and menu-authoring. WYSIWYG layout editor following Windows guidelines.

**Avionics Domain**: Primary Flight Display (PFD) with 3D synthetic view of environment. Navigation Display (ND) with 2D bird's-eye view. Automatic range adaptation, dark cockpit concept, and visual decluttering during warnings. Auditory warning signals augmenting visual channel.
externalInterfaceRequirements.hardwareInterfaces:
**Public Domain**: Information terminal (accesspoint) with solid user interface, microphone, speakers, camera for face detection/recognition, touchscreen or keyboard input.

**Vetronics Domain**: Ruggedized BARCO display with communication via Serial port (RS-232, EIA-422/485), USB, FireWire, IrDA, CAN bus. Vector Unit hardware with symbol generator, OSD capability, multiple video inputs.

**Avionics Domain**: Flight simulation workstation with PFD and ND displays rendered via OpenGL.
externalInterfaceRequirements.softwareInterfaces:
**Consumer Authoring Tool**: Generates C code for target platforms (e.g., DVD player embedded systems). Supports project repositories with font definitions, color palettes, bitmaps, and strings.

**Vetronics UI Editor**: Component-based plug-in architecture using COM/COM+ on Windows (98/ME/2000+). Generates UI code interpretable by firmware Virtual Machine. Upload/download communication modules for embedded systems.

**Avionics**: Multi-agent system using JACK Intelligent Agents (Java) with shared ontology for inter-agent communication. OpenGL for display rendering on Windows NT.
externalInterfaceRequirements.communicationsInterfaces:
**Public Domain**: Network-based content distribution for accesspoints; context-sensitive information retrieval.

**Vetronics Domain**: Multiple communication protocols for UI download to embedded systems: RS-232, EIA-422/485, USB, FireWire, IrDA, CAN bus. System configuration retrieval from Vector Unit.

**Avionics Domain**: Local and global communication buses between warning system agents (TCAS, GPWS, Weather, Resolution agents) via facilitator architecture.

systemFeatures.name: Public Domain Accesspoint Terminal
systemFeatures.description: Multimodal information terminal for public spaces that combines speech, visual, and graphical interfaces with adaptive context understanding. Supports automatic user detection, speaker-independent voice recognition, and natural language understanding. Priority: High.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: Person enters interaction area. Response: System detects user automatically, initiates contact proactively, offers services.
Stimulus: User issues voice command. Response: Context engine interprets command using speech recognition (speaker-independent); graphical display updates to show relevant information.
Stimulus: User reaches dialog dead-end. Response: Help system activates; context engine suggests alternatives ranked by likelihood.
Stimulus: User switches modality (speech to touch). Response: System continues from current dialog state without re-initialization.
functionalRequirements: System must be stable at high-level user application level; avoid failures from user operation or network malfunctions
Dead ends in dialogs handled by special help system with contextual understanding
System guides users quickly and effectively, avoiding cognitive overload from information overflow
Controllable through multiple modalities; modality switching preserves dialog state
Context switches supported at any state; system offers ranked alternatives
Information flow compatible with average user capabilities; 2D graphical model with virtual characters
Speaker-independent voice recognition for all users regardless of age, gender
Automatic user detection without requiring explicit approach; distinguish humans from other entities
Neural network-based context engines for extensibility; derivable from existing rule databases
Open component-based architecture with client-server approach on MS Windows platform
Simulation environment with abstract high-level scripting language interpreter

systemFeatures.name: Consumer UI Authoring Tool
systemFeatures.description: Rapid application development tool for specification, design, simulation, and code generation of on-screen user interfaces for consumer electronics products (e.g., DVD players). Supports off-line adaptivity (flexibility) for iterative UI development. Priority: High.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: Developer creates new UI project. Response: Tool provides project repository with font definitions, color palette, bitmap library, and string resources.
Stimulus: Developer designs screen layout. Response: WYSIWYG editor with standard widgets (screen, dialog, text, button, slider, picture) allows visual composition.
Stimulus: Developer requests simulation. Response: Tool simulates user interface on PC with interactive navigation.
Stimulus: Developer generates code. Response: Tool compiles C code for target consumer platform (e.g., DVD player).
functionalRequirements: Provide standard widget set: screen, dialog, text, button, slider, picture
Provide project repository with font definitions, color palette, bitmap images, strings
Allow color palette management and bitmap import from external sources
Support UI simulation on PC for early validation
Provide complex menu widget with menu structure and navigation function
Provide menu editor for defining menu items
Generate C code for target platforms (e.g., DVD player)
Easy modification of user interface behavior (flexibility/off-line adaptivity)
Support easy development of adaptive user interfaces
Reduce development lead-time significantly compared to paper-based specification
Future extension: wizard to assist user in authoring process

systemFeatures.name: Vetronics UI Editor and Firmware
systemFeatures.description: Plug-in component-based UI editor for ruggedized vehicle-electronics displays with event-driven firmware architecture. Supports off-line adaptivity through UI code download to embedded systems via virtual machine. Includes symbol generator, OSD, touchscreen, and multi-protocol communication. Priority: High.
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: Engineer launches UI Editor. Response: Framework loads with available plug-ins representing hardware capabilities (OSD, Symbol Generator, TouchScreen, Communication protocols).
Stimulus: Engineer creates menu with event-action associations. Response: WYSIWYG editor allows defining overlay elements, hotspot zones, and binding events to actions.
Stimulus: Engineer compiles and downloads UI. Response: Editor generates code, communicates with Vetronics system via selected protocol (Serial/USB/CAN), downloads for firmware VM interpretation.
Stimulus: Emergency condition detected by firmware. Response: Decision agent overrides user-defined UI with emergency display elements (warnings, error messages) based on content prioritization.
functionalRequirements: Optimal video performance on popular video signals without artifacts
Environmental specification compliance (vibration, temperature, shock, humidity, magnetic fields)
Event-driven firmware architecture with UI-interpreter virtual machine
Virtual machine closely matched with firmware for performance
Firmware controls all available hardware; state accessible and exposed to UI code
UI Editor allows flexible creation/modification of UI in interactive, visual way
Communication with Vetronics hardware via Serial (RS-232, EIA-422/485), USB, FireWire, IrDA, CAN
System configuration retrieval from Vector Unit
Support definition of system behavior through event-action associations on UI elements
Open plug-in framework using COM/COM+ component-based architecture
System Configuration Wizard for setup
Font authoring modalities for custom typography
Menu authoring combining OSD, Symbol Generator, HotSpots, and Event-Action associations
Compile step for code generation and download to embedded system
Decision agent for content prioritization between UI and emergency data
WYSIWYG approach with Windows guidelines for editor usability
Simulation module for offline testing without physical Vetronics system
Symbol Generator functionality with macro definition, text, and bitmap operations

systemFeatures.name: Intelligent Adaptive Flight Deck
systemFeatures.description: Multi-agent intelligent system for aircraft cockpit that adapts displays to pilot situation, state, and task. Integrates GPWS and TCAS warning systems with negotiated resolution advisories. Supports six levels of adaptation from full automation to user-controlled. Uses 3D synthetic PFD and adaptive 2D Navigation Display. Priority: Critical (safety-related).
stimulusResponseSequences: Stimulus: GPWS detects terrain proximity. Response: Warning System Group agents communicate; ND range adapts to show terrain threat; non-critical display features darkened or removed; integrated auditory warning issued.

Stimulus: TCAS detects collision risk. Response: TCAS agent and Resolution agent negotiate advisory that doesn't conflict with terrain avoidance; PFD shows 3D synthetic view of traffic/terrain; ND highlights threat.
Stimulus: Pilot deviates from intended flight plan. Response: System detects slip by comparing goals, intentions, and predicted flight state; provides recommendation to correct error.
Stimulus: Multiple simultaneous hazards. Response: Multi-agent system negotiates resolution advisories across GPWS, TCAS, and Weather agents; presents optimized alternatives for pilot decision.
functionalRequirements: Establish and maintain pilot situation awareness by presenting right information in right format at right time
Detect and correct errors by comparing system goals, pilot intentions, actual and predicted flight state
Decision making support by proposing optimized alternatives for pilot selection
Multi-agent architecture with shared ontology for inter-agent communication
Integration of GPWS and TCAS on system level with data exchange at all functional levels
Warning system agents exchange: environmental information, hazard detection, resolution advisories
Automatic ND range adaptation to show warning causes
Dark cockpit concept: non-relevant features darkened or removed during threats
Support all six adaptation levels: self-adaptation, user-controlled self-adaptation, user-initiated self-adaptation, computer-aided adaptation, system-initiated adaptation, user adaptation
Pilot always offered opportunity to overrule self-adaptation
System awareness transparency: pilots can 'see through' automation logic
Non-intrusive adaptation supporting visual momentum
Auditory warning signal representing integrated warning system resolution
3D synthetic PFD with environmental visualization
2D bird's-eye ND with adaptive content management
Multi-agent subsystem for managing display content and inferring user intent
Programmed in Java using JACK Intelligent Agents with OpenGL displays on Windows NT

performanceRequirements: Public terminal: real-time speech recognition and face detection for immediate user interaction
Vetronics firmware: optimal video performance without artifacts on popular signals
Avionics displays: real-time 3D rendering at flight simulation rates via OpenGL
Consumer authoring tool: significant reduction in UI development lead-time vs paper-based methods
safetyRequirements: Avionics: adaptive flight deck must increase flight safety (65% of incidents caused by human error)
Avionics: self-adaptation in time-critical situations may be mandatory; occurrence must be well-known and trained
Avionics: pilot must always have opportunity to overrule system adaptation
Vetronics: decision agent must prioritize emergency data over user-defined UI in dangerous conditions
Vetronics: potential for simulation-based UI certification for life-critical conditions
securityRequirements: Public terminal: privacy-sensitive output channel preferences for user interaction
Public terminal: user detection must distinguish humans from other entities
softwareQualityAttributes: Extensibility: all domains require architectures supporting future feature additions
Usability: validated through expert reviews, questionnaires, log analysis, and video surveillance
Maintainability: Vetronics requires plug-in architecture to avoid monolithic update issues
Interoperability: component-based and open architecture standards preferred across domains
Adaptivity: both online (runtime) and offline (development-time) adaptation supported
businessRules: Two-milestone project: first prototype demonstrates basic functionality; second prototype incorporates lessons learned
Domain-oriented organization with cross-cutting workpackage themes
Usability engineering distributed across partners due to funding loss for dedicated WP4 partners
Individual armed services/companies define their specific platform requirements

otherRequirements: Common Adaptivity Reference Framework (D3) used across domains for structuring adaptive behavior
Longitudinal usability studies acknowledged as needed but constrained by 2-year project timeline
Component-based plug-in architectures envisioned in at least two domains for final demonstrators
Neural network context engines planned for public terminal speech understanding extensibility

glossary.definition: User Centred Intelligence — ITEA 99002 EUREKA 2023 research project on adaptive multimodal user interfaces across four application domains
Information Technology for European Advancement — the program framework for the BEYOND consortium
The ability of a system to automatically adjust its behavior, presentation, or interaction style based on user context, preferences, and environmental conditions at runtime
Off-line adaptivity — the ability to create and modify user interfaces during the development phase using tools, then deploy to target systems
Support for multiple input/output channels (speech, visual, touch, graphical) that complement each other and can be switched without dialog state loss
A multimodal, adaptive public information terminal/kiosk developed by APC Interactive Solutions AG for public space applications
Vehicle-electronics — computer systems embedded in special-purpose vehicles (ships, trains, trams, airplanes, construction vehicles) requiring ruggedized displays
The BARCO Vetronics display system hardware with firmware supporting OSD, symbol generator, and virtual machine for UI code interpretation
Ground Proximity Warning System — aircraft warning system that detects terrain proximity hazards
Traffic alert and Collision Avoidance System — aircraft system that detects potential mid-air collisions
Primary Flight Display — cockpit display providing 3D synthetic view of the flight environment
Navigation Display — cockpit display showing 2D bird's-eye view of the navigation environment
Display concept where non-critical information is automatically darkened or removed during warning/alert situations to reduce cognitive load
JACK Intelligent Agents — multi-agent platform by Agent Oriented Software Pty. Ltd. used for implementing intelligent agent systems in Java
On-Screen Display — graphics overlay displayed on top of video content for menus, status, and navigation
Virtual Machine — firmware component in Vetronics that interprets UI code generated by the UI Editor and interacts with hardware
