introduction.purpose:
This Software Requirements Specification (SRS) clearly and precisely describes the requirements of the **HATS-GUI**, a Graphical User Interface (GUI) for the **High Assurance Transformation System (HATS)**. The HATS-GUI is a platform-independent GUI to simplify user interaction with HATS, a formal methods tool developed by the High Integrity Software (HIS) program at Sandia National Laboratories for transformation-based software development.

The SRS divides system requirements into two major sections: **behavioral requirements** (interaction between system and environment) and **non-behavioral requirements** (attributes of the product as it performs its functions). This document serves as a reference guide to the developer for software design, implementation, and maintenance, and as an agreement on requirements between the customer, guidance team, and software development team.

Text highlighted in gray refers to changes since the previous version (1.4) of this document.
introduction.documentConventions: Requirements are identified with the notation [SRSreq NN] where NN is a sequential number from 01 to 166. Deleted requirements are marked as '<deleted>' but retain their number for traceability. The SRS follows IEEE 830-1998 structure with Introduction, General Description, Specific Requirements, and Appendices. Use cases follow a numbered scenario format with alternatives marked as 'ALT <n>' where steps in the alternative replace steps in the main scenario. Step numbers in alternatives indicate which main scenario steps are being replaced (e.g., 'A1-2A' = Alternative 1, first step replacing step 2).
introduction.intendedAudience: Dr. Victor Winter (Customer, Sandia National Laboratories), Guidance Team (Dr. Ann Gates, Dr. Steve Roach, Francisco Javier Leyva — University of Texas at El Paso), and the five software development teams (Creative Team, Omega Team, Porcelain Team, Resilient Team, Suzaku Team — CS4311 Fall 2001 course students).
introduction.productScope:
The **HATS-GUI** is a platform-independent graphical user interface for the High Assurance Transformation System (HATS). HATS is a formal methods tool that performs program transformations in a provably correct fashion, enabling users to construct software by transforming a target program written in an abstract language to a transformed output program via high assurance transformations that preserve program semantics.

The HATS-GUI provides:
1. **Management of applications** — creation, deletion, modification of application files, managing file associations
2. **Parsing of transformation language programs and target files** — orchestrating parsing via HATS-SML services
3. **Execution of transformation language programs** — applying transformations to target files
4. **Examination of SDT and pretty-printed output** — displaying, navigating, searching transformation outputs

An **application** consists of a core domain (lexical specification, grammar, user-defined functions), transformation language programs, target files, output files, file associations, and user-selected configurations.

A previous prototype exists but is inadequate due to lack of portability and inability to display outputs adequately. The goal is to make the system more user-friendly, platform independent, and increase HATS usage within the transformation and research community.
references: [1] Creative/Omega/Porcelain/Resilient/Suzaku Software, Interview Report. February 2001
[2] Creative/Omega/Porcelain/Resilient/Suzaku Software, Feasibility Report. February 2001
[3] Creative/Omega/Porcelain/Resilient/Suzaku Software, Prototype Report. March 2001
[4] Creative/Omega/Porcelain/Resilient/Suzaku Software, Draft SRS. May 2001
[5] Roach, S., Communications Protocol Memorandum, 2/22/2001
[6] Roach, S., Memorandum from Dr. Winter, 2/28/2001
[7] Omega Software Solutions, Personal Communication Notes on Dr. Winter Q&A, April 20, 2001
[8] Winter, V., An Overview of HATS: A Language Independent High Assurance Transformation System, 1999
[9-22] Various email communications and personal communications between Leyva, Roach, Winter, and Fuehrer, March-October 2001

overallDescription.productPerspective:
HATS was developed by the HIS program at Sandia National Laboratories to explore transformation-based software development. Transformations are a well-known formal method for developing software. HATS performs program transformations in a provably correct fashion — users construct software by transforming a target program written in an abstract language to a transformed output program in a more concrete language. By demonstrating that transformations preserve semantics of the target program, the user has assurance that the transformed program is correct.

**HATS Architecture**: HATS applies a sequence of transformations to a target program by following instructions in a transformation language program. The transformation language program consists of sequences of transformation instructions and constructs to control the application of transformations. The architecture includes: Target Parser → Target SDT, Program Parser → Program SDT, HATS Rewriting Engine → Output SDT, and Prettyprinter → Output Text.

**HATS-SML** is a collection of ML programs providing the parsing, transforming, and pretty-printing functions. HATS-SML has been ported to most common computer platforms. The HATS-GUI interfaces with HATS-SML by starting programs and passing command line arguments.

HATS is publicly available, and the HATS-GUI will be delivered with HATS.
productFunctions: Management of applications: create, delete, modify application files; manage file associations between target files, transformation language programs, style files, editors, and outputs
Parsing of transformation language programs (.tlp) and target files (.tgt) via HATS-SML services to generate Syntax Derivation Trees (SDTs)
Execution of transformation language programs: apply transformation sequences to target programs via HATS-SML's ApplyTransformations program
Display and manipulation of SDT outputs as directed acyclic graphs with expand/collapse/hide/unhide/rehide functionality, configurable node colors and shapes, navigation windows with locator boxes
Display and manipulation of pretty-printed text output with search, select, and copy capabilities
Script file execution: sequential execution of HATS-SML programs from a script file with minimal user intervention
Synchronized display of SDT and pretty-printed text from common target file with mirrored navigation, selection, and highlighting
Error display and collection from HATS-SML standard error and standard output streams
Configurable application settings: editors, node display (colors/shapes), file associations, expand levels, HATS-SML startup commands

userClassesAndCharacteristics.characteristics: Expert in a given problem domain with high level of understanding of transformations as a tool for constructing software. Computer savvy, possessing experience with editors, software tools, hierarchical file systems, and menu navigation. A limited amount of direction and assistance will be necessary. May set up an application for another user. Primary activities: prepare applications, apply transformations to target files, view transformation output.

overallDescription.operatingEnvironment: Platform independent — designed to function on any operating system supporting Java and ML. Specifically tested under Sun Solaris 8, Windows 2000, and Linux. Intended to run on any variation of Microsoft Windows later than Windows 95. Requires a Java Virtual Machine (JVM 1.3) installed, an ML interpreter installed, and HATS-SML version 2.0 running on the same system. Host OS must support a hierarchical, tree-structured file system.
designAndImplementationConstraints: [SRSreq 165] System shall be implemented in Java 1.3 programming language
[SRSreq 164] GUI shall run on Windows 2000, Sun Solaris 8, and Linux without modification of Java code
[SRSreq 166] Prohibited algorithm: Draw SDT into large virtual window with smaller viewing area + zoom — results in excessive memory usage and slow performance
System shall minimize number of windows opened (not acceptable to open new window for every output file)
System shall be completed by December 2001
JVM must be installed on the system
ML interpreter must be installed on the system
HATS-SML version 2.0 must be available by October 2001
HATS supports only one user at a time
Host OS must support hierarchical, tree-structured file system
userDocumentation: This SRS (HATS GUI SRS 1.5) serves as the definitive reference
Interview Report, Feasibility Report, and Prototype Report from the five software teams
HATS-SML Communication Protocol (Appendix D)
Individual Software Design Documents to be developed during implementation
assumptionsAndDependencies: JVM shall have been installed on the system
ML interpreter shall have been installed on the system
HATS shall run on the same system on which the HATS-GUI is running
HATS shall support only one user at a time
Users shall have a high level of sophistication and not need extensive help messages
The Host OS shall support a hierarchical, tree-structured file system
The development team shall use this SRS to implement the system
HATS-SML version 2.0 shall be available by October 2001

externalInterfaceRequirements.userInterfaces:
**[SRSreq 01]** Any operation requiring a file name shall allow the user to select from a list or key in a file name, relative path, or complete path.
**[SRSreq 02]** For any operation where user is prompted to select from a list, user shall be able to cancel.
**[SRSreq 03]** When collecting generated output files from HATS-SML, the HATS-GUI shall overwrite files whose names are generated automatically.
**[SRSreq 04]** When saving or copying files: prompt for file name → examine directory → if file exists, prompt to overwrite → write using temp name → delete old → rename new. Error: leave file unchanged, notify user.
**[SRSreq 05]** Output pretty-printed text files and SDTs shall only be displayed when user requests.
**[SRSreq 06]** Main interface shall be menu driven with five top-level menus: **File** (Select, Edit, Save-as, Delete, Exit), **Application** (Select, Close, Save, Save-as, Delete), **Configure** (Editors, Node Display, File Associations, HATS-SML), **Run** (Generate Parser, Parse Target, Parse Program, Execute Transforms, Pretty-Print), **View** (SDT, Text, Standard error, Standard output, Select Text, Copy Text).
**[SRSreq 07-39]** Detailed menu item requirements for each menu entry including File operations, Application management, Configuration settings, Run commands, and View output options.
**[SRSreq 24]** User shall specify number of levels of nodes to expand during SDT viewing.
**[SRSreq 25]** Minimum eight colors for displayed nodes: red, yellow, white, black, green, blue, orange, violet.
**[SRSreq 26]** Minimum two shapes for displayed nodes: rectangles and ellipses.
externalInterfaceRequirements.softwareInterfaces:
**HATS-SML Interface [SRSreq 40-42]:**
- Interface with HATS-SML version 2.0 for parsing, executing transformations, creating output SDTs, error messages, pretty-printed text, and parsed transformation language programs.
- Interface consists of starting HATS-SML programs and passing command line arguments, capturing standard output and standard error streams.
- HATS-SML programs: MakeParser, ParseTarget, ParseTLP, FindTargets, ApplyTransformations, PrettyPrint (detailed in Appendix D).
- [SRSreq 41] GUI shall allow user to create/modify/copy/delete regardless of HATS-SML status.
- [SRSreq 42] If HATS-GUI fails to start HATS-SML, display 'Unable to start HATS-SML' with Host OS details.

**Host OS Interface [SRSreq 43-49]:**
- [SRSreq 43] Communicate with Sun Solaris, Linux, and Windows 2000.
- [SRSreq 44] Data sets saved as files with appropriate extensions (see file type table).
- [SRSreq 45] User shall be able to initiate processes from HATS-GUI interface with optional command line arguments.
- [SRSreq 46] After initiating editor processes, HATS-GUI not responsible for further communication.
- [SRSreq 47] File services: create, delete, open, read, write, close files.
- [SRSreq 48] Find files in directories for file list displays.
- [SRSreq 49] Compare file time stamps with one-second precision.
externalInterfaceRequirements.communicationsInterfaces: Communication between HATS-GUI and HATS-SML is via process invocation with command line arguments and stream capture. The HATS-GUI starts HATS-SML programs as separate processes via the Host OS, passes input/output file paths as positional command line arguments, and captures standard output and standard error streams. Process exit codes (0 = success, non-zero = failure) are collected by the HATS-GUI [SRSreq 93]. Detailed protocol in Appendix D.

systemFeatures.name: Application Management
systemFeatures.description: Create, open, close, save, save-as, and delete applications. Each application consists of a core domain (lexical specification .spec, grammar .grm, user-defined functions .sml), transformation language programs (.tlp), target files (.tgt), parsed files (.tlp.parsed, .tgt.parsed), pretty-print style files (.sty), output SDTs (.sdt), output text (.txt), script files (.scr), and configuration. All application files stored in one directory named with the application name.
stimulusResponseSequences: Use Case 3 (Select Application): System requests file list from Host OS → displays applications → user selects → system sets current application. ALT: no applications exist, or user navigates directories.

Use Case 5 (Prepare Application): 5 scenarios — Close (with save prompt), Save, Save As (create new directory, copy files), Edit Files (launch external editor based on configuration/associations), Create New Application.
functionalRequirements: [SRSreq 50] Allow user to create an application minimally consisting of lexical specification (.spec), grammar (.grm), user-defined functions, and configuration. User shall name the application.
[SRSreq 51] All files associated with a given application shall be stored in one directory.
[SRSreq 52] Application directory shall be named with the application name.
[SRSreq 53] Exactly one lexical specification file (.spec) per application.
[SRSreq 54] Exactly one grammar file (.grm) per application.
[SRSreq 55] Exactly one user-defined functions file (.lib, written in ML) per application.
[SRSreq 57] HATS-GUI shall suggest file names: same name for different directory copies, sequentially numbered extension for same directory (e.g., NatLang.txt → NatLang.txt.2).

[SRSreq 58] Application shall have associated configuration: file associations (10 types defined in Table 6), editor configurations, editor associations (by file type and specific file), node display (colors/shapes), node expansion level, parser file name.

[SRSreq 69-79] Menu item responses for File/Select, File/Save As, File/Delete (with confirmation), File/Edit (editor association cascade: file→file type→default→prompt→configure), File/Exit (kill HATS-SML processes, save prompt), Application/Select (directory navigation), Application/Close (save prompt), Application/Save, Application/Save As (copy directory), Application/Delete (with subdirectory confirmation).
[SRSreq 80] Configure menu provides list of configuration elements for user modification.
[SRSreq 94] Allow user to import a file into the selected application by copying into application directory.

systemFeatures.name: Parsing and Transformation Execution
systemFeatures.description: Orchestrate parsing of transformation language programs and target files via HATS-SML. Execute transformation language programs by applying transformations to targets. Support script file execution for sequential automated processing.
stimulusResponseSequences: Use Case 1 (Parse): User selects parse option → selects file → system checks parser timestamps → if parser outdated, regenerates via MakeParser → sends file to ParseTarget or ParseProgram → HATS-SML generates SDT. ALT: parse target instead of program, file already selected, no parser exists, parser outdated, parser creation error, parse error.

Use Case 2 (Execute Transformation): User selects execute → selects TLP → checks parser → checks parsed program → clears displays → starts ApplyTransformations → captures output/error streams. ALT: file selected, no parser, parser outdated, no parsed program, parsed program outdated.

Use Case 9 (Generate Pretty-printed Output): User selects pretty-print → selects target SDT → selects pretty-print style file → generates output filename (.txt) → starts PrettyPrint → HATS-SML applies style to SDT → writes formatted text. ALT: already selected, style associated, file exists (overwrite prompt), cancel, error.
functionalRequirements: [SRSreq 81] Run/Generate Parser: generate parser using MakeParser HATS-SML program.
[SRSreq 82] Run/Parse Target: parse selected target program using ParseTarget.
[SRSreq 83] Run/Parse Program: parse selected transformation language program using ParseTLP.
[SRSreq 84] Run/Execute Transforms: execute ApplyTransformations.
[SRSreq 85] Run/Pretty-print: execute PrettyPrint.
[SRSreq 95] Use protocol and program signatures listed in Appendix D when executing HATS-SML programs.

[SRSreq 96] Transformation execution sequence: confirm TLP exists → check parser younger than grammar/spec → check parsed program younger than parser/TLP/library → run FindTarget → for each target, check parsed version → start ApplyTransformations with parsed TLP, library, output file → redirect and capture stdout/stderr.
[SRSreq 98] Allow user to continue working while transformations are taking place (edit files, view output, submit other problems).
[SRSreq 99] Allow user to execute script files containing linear sequences of HATS-SML programs, executing sequentially until error or completion.
[SRSreq 100] Prior to script execution, clear standard error and standard output display screens.
[SRSreq 93] Collect process exit codes from HATS-SML (0 = nominal, non-zero = failure).

systemFeatures.name: SDT Display and Navigation
systemFeatures.description: Display Syntax Derivation Trees (SDTs) as directed acyclic graphs with expand, collapse, hide, unhide, rehide, search, and navigation capabilities. Support configurable node colors, shapes, and expansion levels. Provide navigation windows with locator boxes for large SDTs.
stimulusResponseSequences: Use Case 7 (Display SDT): 10 scenarios — SDT Display (work area with graph window, text window, scroll bars, navigation window with locator box), Select Nodes (right-click, keyboard+enter, drag box, shift+select → highlight smallest well-formed tree → highlight corresponding text), Navigation Window Navigation, Expand (left-click or select+expand → display N levels of descendants), Collapse, Hide Nodes, Search (enter criteria per Appendix E → find first matching sub-tree → highlight), Repeat Search, Copy Text from Text Window, Refresh Display.
functionalRequirements: [SRSreq 62] Open new window when user selects to display output.
[SRSreq 63] Display windows shall have close buttons.
[SRSreq 64] Navigation window shall be available.
[SRSreq 65] Each active display window shall have a mouse cursor.
[SRSreq 66] Each display window shall have a keyboard cursor.
[SRSreq 67] Keyboard cursor movable by arrow keys.
[SRSreq 68] Keyboard cursor set to mouse cursor location on click.
[SRSreq 86] View/SDT menu: prompt user to select SDT file, display graph.
[SRSreq 90] Left mouse click on expandable node: expand or collapse.
[SRSreq 91] Number of expansion levels determined by application configuration.
[SRSreq 92] Left click on expanded node collapses it, removing all descendants from display.
[SRSreq 101] Allow user to select output files for display.
[SRSreq 102] Display output files based on type.
[SRSreq 104] Select arbitrary set of displayed nodes via right-click, keyboard, drag rectangle, shift+select.
[SRSreq 105] Deselect all nodes by clicking empty space or pressing escape.
[SRSreq 106] Selecting nodes highlights all displayed nodes in smallest well-formed tree.
[SRSreq 107] Click in navigation window to jump to SDT location.
[SRSreq 109] Dynamically resizable windows.
[SRSreq 110] SDT display includes leaf node text in text display area.
[SRSreq 111] Text area shows concatenation of leaf node labels (left-to-right, in-order). Non-terminal leaves shown in pointed brackets (<TERM>).
[SRSreq 112] Copy text from text display area to system paste buffer.
[SRSreq 113] Display more than one SDT simultaneously in separate windows.

[SRSreq 114-118] Display SDTs as directed acyclic graphs. Initially show root only. Parent displayed higher than children. Children of single node at same height. Configurable node colors and shapes by type.

[SRSreq 119-126] User controls: expand (add N levels), collapse (remove all descendants), hide (remove selected, change parent/descendant types), unhide (reverse all hides), rehide (reverse most recent unhide), fully expand sub-tree regardless of configuration level.
[SRSreq 127] View multiple SDTs simultaneously.
[SRSreq 128] SDT displays provide leaf text view, copy-and-pasteable to other applications.
[SRSreq 129] SDT refresh: erase current display, re-read SDT file, generate new display approximating previous by expanding to same number of nodes.

[SRSreq 130-134] Navigation: scroll bars, arrow-key scrolling, text cursor movement rules (left/right/up/down with line wrapping), graph cursor movement rules (left/right to same-level non-hidden nodes, up to closest non-hidden ancestor, down to leftmost nearest non-hidden descendant), pointing device tracking.
[SRSreq 135] Navigation window with compressed SDT view and locator box showing currently displayed portion.
[SRSreq 136] User can toggle navigation window on/off.

[SRSreq 137-141] SDT search: enter search pattern per Appendix E → find matching sub-tree → highlight matching nodes → center display. Search wraps from end to start. Repeat search forwards and backwards. Ctrl-Insert to copy selected node text, Ctrl-Shift-Insert to paste into search pattern.

systemFeatures.name: Pretty-Printed Text Display
systemFeatures.description: Display and manipulate formatted text output from transformations with search, select, copy capabilities and synchronized highlighting with SDT display.
stimulusResponseSequences: Use Case 8 (Display Pretty-Printed Text): 3 scenarios — Display (work window with scroll bars), Select Text (click-drag or shift+arrow, highlights in both text and SDT windows), Search (enter criteria per Appendix F → find matching portions → highlight, mirror in SDT if both open).
functionalRequirements: [SRSreq 87] View/Text menu: prompt user to select pretty-printed text file.
[SRSreq 144] Formatting faithful to pretty-printed text file. No additional whitespace. Fixed width font.
[SRSreq 145] User can select pretty-printed text files to display.
[SRSreq 146] Selected text displayed in separate window.
[SRSreq 147] Scroll left, right, up, down if text too large for window.
[SRSreq 148] Text search capability per Appendix F: enter pattern → find match → highlight → center display → set keyboard cursor. 'No Match Found' if none.
[SRSreq 149] Text search wraps: starts at keyboard cursor, continues left-to-right top-to-bottom, wraps from end to start.
[SRSreq 108] When both SDT and pretty-printed text from same target are open, highlighting and cursor motion mirrored between windows.

[SRSreq 154] SDT navigation causes pretty-printed text navigation. Searching, selecting, cursor motion mirrored. Selected graph nodes → corresponding terminal node text highlighted in pretty-print window.
[SRSreq 155] Pretty-printed text navigation causes SDT navigation. Text selection → corresponding SDT nodes highlighted.

systemFeatures.name: Error and Output Display
systemFeatures.description: Collect and display error messages and debugging output from HATS-SML processes.
functionalRequirements: [SRSreq 59] Display standard output and standard error in tab-selectable window. Cleared prior to executing individual HATS-SML programs (or prior to script execution for scripts).
[SRSreq 88] View/Standard Error: display all outputs written to stderr since most recent clearing.
[SRSreq 89] View/Standard Output: display all outputs written to stdout since most recent clearing.
[SRSreq 150] HATS-GUI shall not halt as a result of any error messages from HATS-SML.
[SRSreq 151] Collect error messages in single display area (window tab), initially clear, all errors in order received.
[SRSreq 152] Errors not displayed until requested by user.
[SRSreq 153] User can clear error window without restarting HATS-GUI.

performanceRequirements: [SRSreq 156] Display SDT with 10,000 nodes in 5 seconds or less.
[SRSreq 157] Display 1,000,000 node SDTs in same time ± 2 seconds as 10,000 node SDTs.
[SRSreq 158] Allow user to request transformations while HATS-SML is performing transformations or parsing.
[SRSreq 159] Navigate/scroll 10,000 node SDT display within 3 seconds per screen width.
[SRSreq 160] Text search algorithm complexity shall not exceed O(n²) for n nodes.
[SRSreq 161] Tree search algorithm complexity shall not exceed O(n²) for n nodes.
[SRSreq 162] Scrolling algorithm complexity for text and graphs shall not exceed O(n²) for n displayed nodes.
safetyRequirements: No safety requirements identified for this system.
securityRequirements: [SRSreq 163] The user shall not be required to log in or authenticate identity when using the system.
softwareQualityAttributes: Platform independence: run on Windows 2000, Sun Solaris 8, and Linux without Java code modification [SRSreq 164]
Concurrent operation: user can work while transformations execute [SRSreq 98]
Error resilience: HATS-GUI shall not halt on HATS-SML errors [SRSreq 150]
Portability: Java 1.3 implementation, ML + JVM as only dependencies [SRSreq 165]
businessRules: System developed for the HIS program at Sandia National Laboratories to increase HATS usage in the transformation and research community
HATS is publicly available and HATS-GUI will be delivered with HATS
System must be completed by December 2001
Developed through CS4311 Fall 2001 course at UTEP with five software teams

otherRequirements: No other requirements at this time [Section 3.4].

glossary.definition: A set of files describing a problem domain and a (possibly empty) set of transformation language programs, target files, and output files. An application is a stand-alone entity containing all data sets required to perform and view transformations. Includes a set of associations and user-selected configurations.

A tree where nodes represent either the left-hand side of grammar productions used to parse a string or terminal symbols in the grammar. The children of nodes represent the right-hand side of the production used to parse the node.
The visual representation of an SDT or part of an SDT. A set of displayed nodes and the arcs connecting those nodes. Not every SDT node has a corresponding displayed node.

The visual, graphical display of a node. Every displayed node has a corresponding SDT node. Types: Root, Non-expandable, Non-expandable with hidden parent, Expandable, Expandable with hidden parent, Internal.

High Assurance Transformation System — the transformation system developed by HIS at Sandia National Laboratories. Includes both HATS-SML programs and a user interface. Provides services for parsing and running transformation language programs.

A set of service programs (written in ML) providing the parsing, transforming, and pretty-printing functions of HATS. Programs: MakeParser, ParseTarget, ParseTLP, FindTargets, ApplyTransformations, PrettyPrint.

A set of files consisting of one lexical specification file (.spec), one grammar file (.grm), and zero or one user-defined libraries (.sml). Defines the language domain over which transformations occur.
A sequence of transformations along with constructs to control the application of the transformations. Stored in .tlp files.
The program to which HATS applies a transformation. Stored in .tgt files.
A text string output by HATS representing the formatted result from applying a transformation language program to a target file. Formatting done according to the pretty-print style (.sty file).
A program generated from the grammar and lexical specifications to parse transformation language program files and target files. Stored with no extension, generated by MakeParser.

The set of characteristics related to an application that controls HATS-GUI behavior. Elements: File Associations (10 types), Editor Configurations, Editor Associations, Node Display (colors/shapes), Node Expansion level, Parser file name.

A displayed window containing an abstract representation of an entire SDT. Contains a locator box showing the currently displayed portion relative to the entire SDT. Facilitates navigation of large SDTs.

Given a tree T and a set of nodes N in T, the smallest well-formed tree of N is a tree S such that S is a sub-tree of T, every element of N is in S, every descendant of the root of S in T is also an element of S, and S has no proper sub-trees that are smallest well-formed trees of N in S.
A text string used as a single unit (e.g., a word). Tokens are described by the lexical specification and are the smallest unit of SDT search.
A leaf node of a displayed graph whose corresponding tree node has immediate descendants. Can be expanded to show children.
High Integrity Software — the program at Sandia National Laboratories that developed HATS for transformation-based software development.

flowchartDiagram.syntaxExplanation: This diagram shows the HATS architecture data flow from inputs through the HATS rewriting engine to outputs.
flowchartDiagram.code:
flowchart TD
    subgraph Core["Core Domain"]
        SPEC[".spec\nLexical Specification"]
        GRM[".grm\nGrammar"]
        LIB[".sml\nUser-Defined Functions"]
    end

    subgraph Inputs["Input Files"]
        TGT[".tgt\nTarget File"]
        TLP[".tlp\nTransformation\nLanguage Program"]
        STY[".sty\nPretty-Print Style"]
    end

    subgraph HATS_SML["HATS-SML Services"]
        MP["MakeParser"]
        PT["ParseTarget"]
        PP_TLP["ParseTLP"]
        FT["FindTargets"]
        AT["ApplyTransformations"]
        PPR["PrettyPrint"]
    end

    subgraph Outputs["Output Files"]
        PARSER["Parser Program"]
        TGT_SDT[".tgt.parsed\nTarget SDT"]
        TLP_SDT[".tlp.parsed\nProgram SDT"]
        OUT_SDT[".sdt\nOutput SDT"]
        OUT_TXT[".txt\nPretty-Printed Text"]
    end

    SPEC --> MP
    GRM --> MP
    MP --> PARSER
    PARSER --> PT
    TGT --> PT
    PT --> TGT_SDT
    PARSER --> PP_TLP
    TLP --> PP_TLP
    PP_TLP --> TLP_SDT
    TLP --> FT
    FT -->|target list| PT
    TLP_SDT --> AT
    LIB --> AT
    AT --> OUT_SDT
    OUT_SDT --> PPR
    STY --> PPR
    PPR --> OUT_TXT
flowchartDiagram.caption: HATS Architecture and HATS-SML Service Data Flow

stateDiagram.syntaxExplanation: This state diagram shows the HATS-GUI operational states from application selection through transformation and display.
stateDiagram.code:
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NoAppSelected
    NoAppSelected --> AppSelected: select application
    AppSelected --> NoAppSelected: close application
    AppSelected --> SavingApp: save
    SavingApp --> AppSelected: done
    AppSelected --> MakingTargetParser: parse target
    AppSelected --> MakingTLPParser: parse TLP
    MakingTargetParser --> ParsingTargetProgram: parser ready
    MakingTLPParser --> ParsingTLPProgram: parser ready
    ParsingTargetProgram --> AppSelected: done
    ParsingTLPProgram --> AppSelected: done
    AppSelected --> ApplyingTransformation: apply transformation
    ApplyingTransformation --> ApplyingTLP: ready
    ApplyingTLP --> AppSelected: done
    AppSelected --> DisplayingSDT: view SDT
    AppSelected --> PrettyPrint: pretty print
    DisplayingSDT --> AppSelected: close
    PrettyPrint --> AppSelected: done
    NoAppSelected --> [*]: exit
    AppSelected --> [*]: exit
stateDiagram.caption: HATS-GUI State Transition Diagram

dataDictionary.definition: Grammar (.grm): language definition, exactly one per domain. Lexer Specification (.spec): token descriptions, exactly one per domain. User-Defined Functions (.sml): ML function definitions for evaluating function symbols, zero or one per domain. Target (.tgt): input program to transform, any number. Parsed Target (.tgt.parsed): SDT of parsed target, zero or one per target. Transformation Language Program (.tlp): sequence of transformations, any number. Parsed TLP (.tlp.parsed): SDT of parsed TLP, zero or one per TLP. Pretty-Print Style (.sty): formatting directives, any number. Pretty-Printed Output (.txt): formatted result text. SDT Output (.sdt): syntax derivation tree output. Parser: generated from grammar + lexer. Script (.scr): sequence of HATS-SML programs.

MakeParser <lexer> <grammar> <parser-program>: generates parser from lexer and grammar files. ParseTarget <parser-program> <target> <targetSDT>: generates parsed target SDT. ParseTLP <parser-program> <program> <programSDT>: generates parsed TLP SDT. FindTargets <program> <targetlist>: extracts target file names from TLP. ApplyTransformations <functions> <programSDT>: executes transformation, outputs written per TLP-embedded names. PrettyPrint <style> <targetSDT> <pretty-print>: generates formatted text output.

File Associations: 10 types including grammar↔parser, parsed TLP↔TLP, parsed target↔target, output SDT↔TLP, library↔TLP, input targets↔TLP, TLP↔output SDT, 2 most-recent style files↔output SDT, target+style↔text output. Editor Configurations: Host OS startup commands. Editor Associations: by file type and by specific file. Node Display: colors and shapes for 6 node types. Node Expansion: number of levels to expand on click. Parser File: name of parser associated with application language.

A search pattern is a string w such that w is a sub-string of a string α and α is a string derived from some non-terminal β in the target grammar. Search finds the smallest derivation string α containing ω — i.e., the smallest well-formed SDT sub-tree whose leaf text contains the search pattern. Search proceeds top-to-bottom, left-to-right with wrap-around.

$ = matches single character. * = matches any set of characters. ^ = start of line. \\ = inhibits interpretation of following character. [...] = matches one of the characters in brackets. Examples: 'abc def g$h' matches 'abc def geh'; 'abc*' matches 'abc def geh'; '^g' matches 'g' at start of line; '\\$' matches literal '$'; 'a[kbl]c' matches 'abc'.

revisionHistory.description: First draft of the Libra Economy-Driven Cluster Scheduler Software Requirements Specification.
revisionHistory.author: Rajkumar Buyya (Project Owner/Client), Dr. Arif Zaman (Faculty Advisor), Jahanzeb Sherwani, Nosheen Ali, Nausheen Lotia, Zahra Hayat (Project Group)

introduction.purpose:
This SRS describes the requirements and specifications of **Libra**, an Economy-Driven Cluster Scheduler. It explains the functional features of the scheduler, including interface details, design constraints, and related considerations such as performance characteristics. The SRS is intended for users and owners of high-performance clusters, cluster management software, job schedulers, and grid resources.

Libra introduces a **computational economy** approach to cluster scheduling, where resource management is driven by user-specified Quality of Service (QoS) parameters (budget and deadline) rather than traditional system-centric approaches (FIFO ordering by submission time). This represents a paradigm shift from maximizing system CPU utilization to maximizing user utility and satisfaction.
introduction.documentConventions: The SRS follows a structure with Introduction, Overall Description, and Specific Requirements sections. Specific Requirements are organized by process/feature areas. Each functional feature has Introduction, Inputs, Processing, and Outputs sub-sections. Data Flow Diagrams (Appendix B) and Data Dictionary (Appendix C) provide supporting visualization and definitions.
introduction.intendedAudience: Users and owners of high-performance clusters, cluster management software developers, job scheduler designers, grid resource managers, the project team (Rajkumar Buyya as project owner/client, Dr. Arif Zaman as faculty advisor, and four student developers), and the open-source community.
introduction.productScope:
The **Libra Scheduler** is an add-on to the queuing, scheduling and resource managing module of the open-source **Sun Grid Engine (SGE)** cluster management system (previously known as Codine). The scheduler offers a **market-based economy-driven service** for managing batch jobs on clusters by scheduling CPU time according to **user utility** rather than system performance considerations.

Scope boundaries:
- **In scope**: Sequential and embarrassingly parallel batch jobs on a homogeneous Linux cluster. Bid-based proportional resource sharing model with stride scheduling algorithm. Budget and deadline-driven job prioritization.
- **Out of scope**: Real parallel jobs with inter-thread dependencies. Heterogeneous cluster support (noted as future extension). User-to-user resource bargaining (as in grid computing environments like Nimrod/G). Process migration for resource fragmentation reduction. GUI interface (deferred to future version).
- **Deferred**: Detailed pricing/costing policy (will not affect scheduling policies). GUI for all components. Simulation/testing tool decision.
references: [1] R. Buyya, D. Abramson, J. Giddy, Nimrod/G: An Architecture for a Resource Management and Scheduling System in a Global Grid, HPC ASIA 2000, IEEE CS Press, 2000
[2] R. Buyya, D. Abramson, J. Giddy, An Economy Driven Resource Management Architecture for Global Computational Power Grids, PDPTA 2000, Las Vegas, June 2000
[3] R. Buyya, D. Abramson, J. Giddy, An Economy Grid Architecture for Service-Oriented Grid Computing, HCW 2001 with IPDPS 2001, San Francisco, April 2001
[4] R. Buyya, H. Stockinger, J. Giddy, D. Abramson, Economic Models for Management of Resources in Peer-to-Peer and Grid Computing, Monash University
[5] B.N. Chun, D.E. Culler, Market-based proportional resource sharing for clusters, Submitted September 1999

overallDescription.productPerspective:
Libra forms part of a larger system comprising the **Sun Grid Engine (SGE)** cluster management system running on a Linux-based cluster. The system architecture is:

1. **SGE** receives jobs submitted by users
2. **SGE** delegates jobs to **Libra** for scheduling decisions
3. **Libra** determines workstation allocation based on budget, deadline, and execution time
4. **Libra** communicates allocation decision to the **SGE resource manager**
5. **SGE resource manager** dispatches the job to the chosen workstation
6. **Libra** implements the **stride scheduling policy** on jobs executing at workstations

No modifications to the Linux kernel are required. Libra is designed as a separable sub-component of SGE.
productFunctions: Enforce resource allocations according to user-centric priorities (budget and deadline) rather than system metrics
Dynamic scheduling: users who need emergency job completion and are willing to pay higher can get dynamic reallocation even if submitted later or system is heavily loaded
Change resource limits, priorities, privileges and execution order of submitted jobs dynamically
Scalable performance: no degradation with addition of nodes and jobs to the cluster
Configurable scheduling policies modifiable to incorporate QoS parameters
Separable from the cluster management system
Administrative security for scheduling operations
Basic level of job accounting to aid scheduling policies
Maximize user utility (QoS) rather than system CPU utilization
Bid-based proportional resource-sharing model for pricing and allocation
Stride scheduling algorithm for proportional time-slicing enforcement
Accept/reject jobs based on feasibility analysis of budget, deadline, and execution time
Sequential and embarrassingly parallel batch job support
Load balancing across execution hosts

userClassesAndCharacteristics.characteristics: Needs to know the exact nature of submitted jobs (execution time, resources required). Must possess technical know-how to use the interface for submitting jobs. Interacts via Linux command line or SGE interface. Can submit jobs, query costs, monitor status, cancel jobs, check credit balance, and check usage history.

Must be an advanced cluster operator, fully qualified in using Linux, SGE, and the Libra Scheduler. Has all user capabilities plus: check node status, view cluster usage patterns, check all job statuses, check node loads, alter cost structure, alter scheduling policy, cancel/suspend/resume any job.

overallDescription.operatingEnvironment: Linux-based homogeneous cluster. Currently: network of four Pentium-III workstations with 128 MB RAM each, running SGE on top of the Linux operating system. Sun Grid Engine version 5.3 cluster management system. PVM (Parallel Virtual Machine) and MPI (Message Passing Interface) libraries for embarrassingly parallel jobs. No Linux kernel modifications required.
designAndImplementationConstraints: Libra shall be a sub-component of Sun Grid Engine (SGE)
All coding shall be done in standard C
Exhaustive searches of entire combinations of jobs shall not be done — heuristics shall be used
Hardware constraint: Currently limited to four Pentium-III workstations with 128 MB RAM
Open source under GNU General Purpose License
A simulating tool for testing may need to be specially designed
SGE version 5.3 required
Hungarian Naming Convention for all code
userDocumentation: All documentation in accordance with open source requirements under GNU GPL
On-line user documentation in the form of man pages accessible through Linux
assumptionsAndDependencies: Workability of SGE cluster management system modules (process migration, etc.) with Libra scheduling policies is assumed
Basic module of job accounting and payment considerations will be provided as they are not the focus of the scheduler
Users are assumed to have a fair estimate of job execution times to facilitate accept/reject decisions
SGE is responsible for receiving job submissions and delegating to Libra
Pricing mechanism will not affect scheduling policies (they assume given cost and budget)

externalInterfaceRequirements.userInterfaces:
**Cluster User Interface** — Minimal requirements via Linux command line or SGE interface, with commands for:
- Submit jobs with associated deadline, cost, and execution time
- Query the cluster to establish current cost per unit time for new jobs
- Monitor the status of submitted jobs
- Cancel jobs submitted by the user
- Check credit balance
- Check usage history

**Cluster Administrator Interface** — Linux command line or SGE interface, with all user commands plus:
- Check status of each cluster node
- Check usage pattern history of the cluster
- Check status of all submitted jobs
- Check load on each cluster node
- Alter the cost structure of the cluster
- Alter the scheduling policy of the cluster
- Cancel, suspend, and resume any job
externalInterfaceRequirements.hardwareInterfaces:
All hardware interfaces are those of Sun Grid Engine, including:
- CPU usage monitoring
- Memory usage monitoring
- Swap file creation
- Network communication
externalInterfaceRequirements.softwareInterfaces: Libra directly interfaces with the modified **Sun Grid Engine** cluster management system, version 5.3. SGE interfaces with the Linux operating system and required software components. For embarrassingly parallel jobs, the **PVM (Parallel Virtual Machine)** and **MPI (Message Passing Interface)** libraries are used by the cluster.
externalInterfaceRequirements.communicationsInterfaces: Libra utilizes the communications architecture of Sun Grid Engine. No unique communications interfaces above SGE are required.

systemFeatures.name: Submit Job
systemFeatures.description: End-user interface for submitting jobs to the cluster via the Sun Grid Engine interface. Verifies cluster availability for submissions and validates user authentication. The SGE interface is adapted to include additional submission parameters (budget, deadline) not already supported by SGE.
functionalRequirements: Validate whether cluster is accepting job submissions
Verify user authentication (name and authentication id)
Accept job submission parameters: executable location, input data location, output directory, system type, maximum execution time, job type (sequential/embarrassingly parallel), budget, deadline
Output boolean values indicating cluster submission status and user verification status
Interface with SGE for job submission handling (Libra does not interface directly with end users for submission)

systemFeatures.name: View Job Status
systemFeatures.description: Allows users or administrators to view details about submitted jobs and the progress of job execution.
functionalRequirements: Authenticate user via User Id and Password before displaying job status
Display available options about submitted jobs
Show progress of job execution information
Process user selections when viewing different aspects of job information

systemFeatures.name: Delete/Change Job
systemFeatures.description: Enables users and administrators to cancel jobs or change specific job parameters. Budget and deadline parameters are not normally changeable as they are crucial to scheduling. Permitted changes include output directory or delayed deadline.
functionalRequirements: Authenticate user via User Id and Password before allowing delete/change
Remove job from queue on deletion
Update cluster status after job removal: CPU load, node status, queue length, quanta allocated to pending jobs, revised expected finishing deadlines
Restrict changes to non-critical parameters (output directory, delayed deadline) — budget and deadline changes normally prohibited
Revise scheduling decisions about resource allocation for pending jobs and new job submissions

systemFeatures.name: Initialize Job
systemFeatures.description: Prepares the scheduler for scheduling and executing a job after SGE acceptance. Retrieves job details and sets them in variables representing cluster and queue state.
functionalRequirements: Retrieve job details from SGE after job acceptance
Parse and set: Job Id, Job Type (sequential/embarrassingly parallel), Standalone Execution Time, Location of executable and input data sets, System Type, Budget, Deadline
Pass parsed parameters to the scheduling module for accept/reject decision
Assign SGE-generated Job Id to the job

systemFeatures.name: Accept/Reject Job
systemFeatures.description: Determines the feasibility of satisfying the user's QoS requirements given the budget, deadline, and execution time. This is the core admission control function.
functionalRequirements: Input job details: Job Id, Job Type, Standalone Execution Time, executable/input locations, System Type, Budget, Deadline
Input cluster information: CPU load, node status, remaining time of pending jobs
Calculate job priority reflecting the share of CPU time deserved based on budget/deadline criteria
Determine whether job can be completed by deadline given execution time and pending jobs on various nodes
Output boolean accept/reject decision
If rejected, provide suggested deadline or cost as precondition for acceptance

systemFeatures.name: Calculate Scheduling Information
systemFeatures.description: Determines how a job will be scheduled using the **Stride Scheduling Algorithm** to enforce resource allocations (CPU cycles) proportional to user's budget and deadline. Performed once a job is accepted.
functionalRequirements: Allocate tickets to job based on its budget and deadline relative to all other jobs on the execution host
Calculate stride = global stride constant ÷ allocated tickets (higher tickets = shorter stride = more frequent scheduling)
Calculate initial pass = stride (pass tracks scheduling progress)
Output scheduling information: job's tickets, stride, and pass values
Ensure proportional resource allocation: jobs with more tickets get scheduled more frequently

systemFeatures.name: Determine Execution Host
systemFeatures.description: Decides on which node and queue the job will be placed for execution, based on budget, execution time, and deadline. Performs load balancing.
functionalRequirements: Assess load on each execution host
Sort hosts in ascending order by load, choose least-loaded host (load balancing)
Select appropriate queue based on job type (sequential/embarrassingly parallel), budget, and other parameters
Input cluster information: CPU load, node status, remaining time of pending jobs, available memory
Output: chosen execution host and queue

systemFeatures.name: Dispatch Job
systemFeatures.description: Takes the job and inserts it into the queue on the chosen execution host. Separated from host determination because the job may wait before receiving CPU time, allowing other urgent-deadline jobs to be scheduled first.
functionalRequirements: Accept chosen execution host and queue from Determine Execution Host
Decide appropriate timing for job dispatch based on queue state and deadline urgency
Send job to appropriate queue on execution host
Job joins competition for resources in the queue

systemFeatures.name: Update Cluster Status
systemFeatures.description: Updates cluster state every time a job is scheduled for execution or removed from the cluster. Acts as a trigger activated on job scheduling or deletion events.
functionalRequirements: Reserve resources required by the job on the assigned node
Update execution host queue status
Send additional load information to the central information store on the master host
Triggered on both job scheduling and job deletion events
No direct output — performs update operations only

systemFeatures.name: Execute Job
systemFeatures.description: Core scheduling loop using the **Stride Scheduling Algorithm**. Runs continuously while jobs exist in queues, determining time-slicing between all concurrently executing jobs.
functionalRequirements: Examine all jobs on the cluster
Select job with minimum pass value (most scheduling urgency)
Allot the selected job a quantum of CPU time
Advance the selected job's pass by its stride value
Use FIFO job ordering as tiebreaker when multiple jobs have equal pass values
Repeat scheduling loop until no jobs remain in a queue
Output updated cluster information: CPU load, node status, remaining time of pending jobs, available memory

performanceRequirements: Maximum response time for job submission: 1 minute
Maximum number of schedulable jobs limited only by node capacity to fulfill deadlines; no upper limit inherent in the Libra scheduler
Deadline sensitivity: assuming accurate job statistics, all jobs completed with 10% error allowance
Cost sensitivity: under all circumstances, maximum cost payable as submitted by user will be the maximum cost charged
safetyRequirements: No specific safety requirements identified for this system.
securityRequirements: Libra shall ensure privacy of user job status
Libra shall ensure full control over job execution
Alteration of scheduling criteria or actual resource allocation shall not be possible without administrator authority
User authentication (Id and Password) required for job status viewing, deletion, and changes
softwareQualityAttributes: Reliability: No support for job migration for resource fragmentation reduction
Reliability: Maximum 1 bug per KLOC (thousand lines of code)
Reliability: Maximum system outage recovery time less than 5 minutes (Linux boot time)
Supportability: Hungarian Naming Convention for all code
Supportability: GNU General Purpose License coding standards
Scalable: performance shall not degrade with addition of nodes and jobs
Dynamic: able to change resource limits, priorities, privileges, and execution order at runtime
Configurable: scheduling policies modifiable to incorporate QoS parameters
Separable: scheduler separable from cluster management system
businessRules: Libra will be released under a GPL license and will be open-source
Sun Grid Engine (SGE) is a copyright trademark of Sun Microsystems, Inc.
Libra Scheduler is a copyright trademark of Mr. Rajkumar Buyya, Monash University
MPI is a copyright of the University of Chicago and Mississippi State University
PVM is a copyright of the University of Tennessee, Oak Ridge National Laboratory, and Emory University
No component of the Libra project will be purchased — entire project is open source

otherRequirements: Detailed pricing/costing policy requirements will be specified in a later version (will not affect scheduling policies)
GUI for all components deferred until basic functionality is implemented
Simulation/testing tool decision pending — may need to be specially designed if generic tool not freely available

glossary.definition: Connecting two or more computers together to take advantage of combined computational power and resources. A cluster works as an integrated collection of resources providing a single system image spanning all nodes. Used for high-performance applications such as AI expert systems, nuclear simulations, and scientific calculations.

Meeting what users actually want, thus maximizing their utility. In the context of Libra, QoS means scheduling based on user-specified budget and deadline constraints rather than system-centric metrics.

Inclusion of user-specified QoS parameters with jobs so that resource management is based on a user-centric approach rather than a system-centric approach. User constraints (deadline, budget) are more important than system policies (submission time ordering). Currently no holistic scheduling mechanism exists in cluster computing to enable differing QoS levels for different clients.

An infrastructure coupling resources (computers, software, devices) across the Internet, presenting them as a unified integrated single resource. Grid computing refers to using this infrastructure for processing jobs and processes.

An economic model allocating cluster resources between competing users based on their criteria (deadline, budget). Resource allocated is proportional to bid values. Users receive tokens/credits whose value depends on demand and cost at time of usage. Priority evaluated according to deadline/price constraints and system load. Priority drives proportional resource allocation via scheduling policy.

A deterministic scheduling algorithm enforcing resource allocations proportional to a user's priority/share. A pool of tickets represents resource rights. Stride = inverse of ticket count (interval between successive scheduling of a job in CPU time units). Jobs with more tickets have shorter strides → scheduled more frequently → greater resource fraction. Accurately schedules jobs in exact proportions based on ticket allocations.
Moving a job from one computer system to another without restarting the job from the beginning. Libra does not support this for resource fragmentation reduction.
Jobs that must be run on a single node and cannot be split into separate jobs.

Parallel jobs comprised of independent parts that can be split and run on separate nodes as sequential jobs, with minimal inter-job communication. Opposed to real parallel jobs with dependent, interacting threads.
A method of quantifying the system resources used by a job. Libra provides basic accounting to aid scheduling policies.
An open-source cluster management system (previously known as Codine) that handles job submission, resource management, and dispatching. Libra operates as a scheduling add-on to SGE.
A cluster in which nodes/workstations have uniform characteristics (same memory size, hard disk space) and run the same operating system.
In stride scheduling, tickets represent resource rights to shared resources. More tickets = shorter stride = more frequent scheduling = greater resource share.
In stride scheduling, the interval (in CPU time units) between two successive schedulings of a job. Stride = global stride constant ÷ tickets. Inversely proportional to ticket count.

In stride scheduling, a counter tracking a job's scheduling progress. Initially set to the job's stride. Advanced by stride after each quantum allocation. Job with minimum pass is selected next for scheduling.

flowchartDiagram.syntaxExplanation: This diagram shows the Libra scheduler's job lifecycle from submission through execution, including the stride scheduling decision loop.
flowchartDiagram.code:
flowchart TD
    subgraph USER["User/Administrator"]
        SUBMIT["Submit Job\n(via SGE/CLI)"]
        VIEW["View Job Status"]
        DELETE["Delete/Change Job"]
    end

    subgraph SGE["Sun Grid Engine"]
        RECV["Receive Job\nSubmission"]
        DISPATCH_SGE["SGE Resource\nManager"]
    end

    subgraph LIBRA["Libra Scheduler"]
        INIT["Initialize Job\n(parse parameters)"]
        ACCEPT["Accept/Reject Job\n(budget+deadline feasibility)"]
        CALC["Calculate Scheduling Info\n(tickets, stride, pass)"]
        HOST["Determine Execution Host\n(load balancing)"]
        DISP["Dispatch Job\n(queue insertion)"]
        UPDATE["Update Cluster Status"]
        EXEC["Execute Job\n(stride scheduling loop)"]
    end

    SUBMIT --> RECV
    RECV --> INIT
    INIT --> ACCEPT
    ACCEPT -->|Rejected| SUBMIT
    ACCEPT -->|Accepted| CALC
    CALC --> HOST
    HOST --> DISP
    DISP --> UPDATE
    UPDATE --> EXEC
    EXEC -->|quantum alloc| EXEC
    EXEC -->|job complete| UPDATE
    DELETE --> UPDATE
    VIEW --> LIBRA
flowchartDiagram.caption: Libra Scheduler Job Lifecycle and Stride Scheduling Architecture

stateDiagram.syntaxExplanation: This state diagram shows job states from submission through scheduling to completion within the Libra economy-driven scheduler.
stateDiagram.code:
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Submitted: user submits job
    Submitted --> Validating: cluster open + user verified
    Submitted --> Rejected_Submission: cluster closed or invalid user
    Validating --> Initialized: parse job parameters
    Initialized --> Feasibility_Check: accept/reject analysis
    Feasibility_Check --> Rejected: deadline/budget infeasible
    Feasibility_Check --> Accepted: can meet QoS
    Rejected --> [*]: suggest alternate deadline/cost
    Accepted --> Scheduled: calculate tickets/stride/pass
    Scheduled --> HostAssigned: determine least-loaded host
    HostAssigned --> Queued: dispatch to execution queue
    Queued --> Executing: selected by stride algorithm
    Executing --> Queued: quantum expired, pass advanced
    Executing --> Completed: job finished
    Completed --> [*]: update cluster status
    Queued --> Cancelled: user/admin cancels
    Executing --> Cancelled: user/admin cancels
    Cancelled --> [*]: update cluster status
stateDiagram.caption: Libra Job State Transitions with Economy-Driven Scheduling

dataDictionary.definition: Job Id (assigned by SGE), Job Type (sequential or embarrassingly parallel), Standalone Execution Time, Location of executable and input data sets, Standard output location, System Type, Budget (user-allocated tokens/credits), Deadline (user-specified completion time).
CPU Load (per node), Node Status (available/busy), Queue Length, Remaining Time of Pending Jobs, Available Memory (per node), Master Host central information store.

Tickets (resource rights proportional to budget/deadline priority), Stride (global stride constant ÷ tickets — scheduling interval), Pass (scheduling progress counter, initially = stride, advanced by stride after each quantum).
Quanta allocated to pending jobs, Revised expected finishing deadlines, Chosen execution host and queue, Updated CPU load and node status after scheduling events.

User Information: name, authentication id (for job submission). User Id and Password (for viewing status, deleting/changing jobs). Administrator has elevated privileges for cluster management operations.

srs_json.projectTitle: Number Portability Administration Center Service Management System (NPAC SMS)

revisionHistory.description: NANC FRS Version 3.0.23 incorporating National Number Pooling, EDR support, and documentation corrections.
revisionHistory.author: North American Numbering Council (NANC)

revisionHistory.description: Major release adding National Number Pooling as replacement for Midwest Region Number Pooling.
revisionHistory.author: NANC

revisionHistory.description: Release 2.0 with service bureau support and wireless portability enhancements.
revisionHistory.author: NANC

revisionHistory.description: Initial NANC version adapted from ICC Subcommittee FRS v1.1.5.
